# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
> **Differentiate between contact and non-contact type temperature sensors. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Contact Type | Non-Contact Type |
|---------|--------------|-------------------|
| Physical contact with process | Yes | No |
| Measurement basis | Conduction of heat into sensor | Radiation emitted/reflected by target |
| Response time | Slower (due to thermal mass) | Very fast (light speed) |
| Range | -200 to ~1000 °C | ~600 to 3500 °C |
| Accuracy | High (0.1 to 1 °C) | Moderate (depends on emissivity) |
| Target suitability | Stationary, accessible | Moving, rotating, hazardous, very hot |
| **Examples** | Mercury-in-glass, Pt-100 RTD, Thermocouple, Thermistor, Bimetallic | Optical pyrometer, Total radiation pyrometer, Infrared pyrometer |

> **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
> **State the Law of Intermediate Metals for thermocouples. Why is it important in practice?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Law of Intermediate Metals** states that *if a third metal C is introduced into a thermocouple circuit (made of metals A and B), the net Seebeck EMF remains unchanged, provided that the two new junctions formed with metal C are at the same temperature.*

**Importance:**
- It allows the use of **measuring instruments, connecting leads, and solder joints** in the thermocouple loop without introducing any error, as long as those junctions are at the *same* temperature.
- It is the theoretical justification for **soldering, welding, or using terminal blocks** in thermocouple circuits — these are essential for practical installation.
- It is the foundation for constructing **thermopiles** (multiple thermocouples in series), where intermediate connecting wires do not affect the EMF.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the principle, construction, and working of a **Total Radiation Pyrometer** with a neat sketch. Derive the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** that governs its operation.

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Principle:**
A total radiation pyrometer measures the *total* radiant energy (across all wavelengths) emitted by a hot body. The output is proportional to the fourth power of the absolute temperature, as given by the Stefan-Boltzmann Law.

**Construction (Féry Radiation Pyrometer — see Section 4.7 diagram):**
1. **Fluorite (CaF₂) lens** — focuses the radiant energy from the target. Fluorite is used because it transmits both visible and infrared wavelengths (glass would absorb IR).
2. **Adjustable diaphragm** — defines the cone of radiation reaching the detector.
3. **Blackened thermopile** — the radiation detector. It consists of several (typically 4 to 16) miniature thermocouples connected in series, with their hot junctions blackened to maximize absorption. The cold junctions are shielded and held at ambient.
4. **Eyepiece with mirror** — used for sighting and aligning the instrument on the target.
5. **Reference temperature compensation** — built-in to cancel the cold-junction effect.

**Working:**
1. The instrument is sighted on the hot target.
2. The fluorite lens collects the radiation and focuses it on the thermopile.
3. The blackened junctions absorb the radiation and heat up.
4. The temperature difference between hot and cold junctions generates an EMF (via the Seebeck effect inside the thermopile).
5. The EMF is amplified and displayed on a calibrated meter in °C.
6. The instrument constant relates EMF to $\varepsilon \sigma T^4$.

**Derivation of Stefan-Boltzmann Law** (refer to Section 3.5 for full derivation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
M(T) &\;=\; \int_0^{\infty} M_\lambda(T)\,d\lambda \\
&\;=\; \frac{c_1\,T^4}{c_2^4}\int_0^{\infty} \frac{x^3}{e^x - 1}\,dx \\
&\;=\; \frac{2\pi^5 k^4}{15 c^2 h^3}\,T^4 \;\equiv\; \sigma T^4
\end{aligned}
$$

For a real surface: $q = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ where $\varepsilon$ is the emissivity and $\sigma = 5.6703 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴.

---

> **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of a **Mercury-in-Glass Thermometer**. Discuss its advantages, limitations, and typical industrial applications.

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Sketch** (textual representation):
```
        ┌──────────────────┐ ← Top (sealed)
        │ ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░ │ ← Inert gas (N₂) space
        │ ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░ │ 
        │ ████████████████ │ ← Mercury column
        │ ████████████████ │ 
        │ ███┌──┐█████████ │ ← Constriction
        │ ███│  │█████████ │
        │ ███│  │█████████ │ ← Capillary bore
        │ ███│  │█████████ │
        │ ███│  │█████████ │
        └────┘  └──────────┘ ← Bulb (mercury reservoir)
       Scale  Engraved   Stem
       Marks  on Glass   
```

**Construction (detailed):**
- **Bulb** — thin-walled glass reservoir containing mercury
- **Stem** — capillary tube of uniform fine bore
- **Scale** — etched on the stem or printed on a backing strip
- **Inert gas** (N₂ or Ar) — fills the space above the mercury to prevent boiling
- **Constriction** — small narrow section above the bulb; prevents mercury from flowing back, allowing the thermometer to be read after removal from the process
- **Expansion chamber** — at the top, accommodates overflow at high temperatures

**Working:**
1. The bulb is placed in contact with the process.
2. Heat conducts through the glass into the mercury.
3. Mercury expands (apparent cubic expansion coefficient $\gamma_a \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$ /K).
4. The expanded mercury rises in the capillary.
5. The column height is read against the calibrated scale.

**Advantages:**
- Simple, no power supply needed
- High accuracy (±0.1 °C for laboratory grade)
- Direct, unambiguous reading
- Inexpensive
- Stable calibration

**Limitations:**
- Fragile (glass)
- Slow response (large thermal mass)
- Mercury is toxic — environmental hazard
- Range limited to **-38 to +357 °C** (Hg freezes at -38.83 °C, boils at 356.7 °C)
- Cannot be used for remote indication
- Cannot measure point temperatures easily

**Industrial Applications:**
- Laboratory measurements
- Calibration of other instruments
- HVAC and building services
- Weather stations
- Clinical (medical) thermometers
- Tank/vessel temperature spot-checks

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

> **(a) [7 Marks]** What is the **Seebeck Effect**? With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **Thermocouple**. List the standard ISA thermocouple types and their temperature ranges.

**(a) Model Solution:**

**Seebeck Effect:**
The Seebeck effect (discovered by Thomas Johann Seebeck in 1821) is the phenomenon in which an **electromotive force (EMF)** is generated at the junction of two dissimilar conductors when their junctions are held at *different* temperatures. The magnitude of this EMF is a function of the temperature difference and the materials of the two conductors.

**Construction & Working (with sketch):**

```
                    A
                   ┌──┐
                   │  │ ← Metal A (e.g., Chromel)
                   │  │
              HJ   │  │
       ┌──────────┘  └──────────┐
       │   ●Hot Junction        │ ← At Temperature T_H
       │   (Measuring Junction) │
       └──────────┬─────────────┘
                  │  ┌────────┐
                  │  │ Metal B (e.g., Alumel)
                  └──┘ ← Cold Junction (At T_C)
                       ↓
                Connected to millivolt meter
                Cold-junction compensation applied
                Indicator reads T_H
```

**Construction elements:**
- **Hot junction (measuring junction)** — the welded or twisted junction exposed to the process
- **Two dissimilar metal wires** (legs) — forming the thermocouple pair
- **Cold (reference) junction** — held at a known temperature (originally 0 °C in ice-bath setups; now compensated electronically)
- **Thermocouple extension/compensation wires** — carry the EMF to the indicator
- **Protective sheath** — metal or ceramic, isolating the wires from the process

**Working:**
1. The hot junction is exposed to the process temperature $T_H$.
2. The cold junction is held at reference $T_C$.
3. A Seebeck EMF is generated: $E = \int_{T_C}^{T_H} (S_B - S_A)\,dT$.
4. This EMF is measured by a millivolt meter or temperature transmitter.
5. The instrument applies cold-junction compensation and linearization to display $T_H$ in °C.

**Standard ISA Thermocouple Types** (memorize — see Section 1.3.5 table):

| Type | Materials | Range (°C) | Sensitivity (μV/°C) | Common Use |
|------|-----------|-------------|----------------------|-------------|
| J | Fe / Constantan | -210 to +760 | ~50 | Reducing atmospheres |
| K | Chromel / Alumel | -270 to +1260 | ~40 | Most common, general |
| T | Cu / Constantan | -270 to +400 | ~40 | Cryogenic, food, lab |
| E | Chromel / Constantan | -270 to +870 | ~60 | Highest sensitivity |
| R | Pt-13% Rh / Pt | -50 to +1480 | ~10 | Laboratory standard |
| S | Pt-10% Rh / Pt | -50 to +1480 | ~10 | Laboratory standard |
| B | Pt-30% Rh / Pt-6% Rh | 0 to +1700 | ~9 | Very high temperature |
| N | Nicrosil / Nisil | -270 to +1260 | ~38 | Improved K |

---

> **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the **working principle of a Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD)**. Derive the **Callendar-Van Dusen equation** and state the IEC 60751 coefficients for a Pt-100 RTD.

**(b) Model Solution:**

**Working Principle:**
The resistance of a pure metal increases with temperature due to increased lattice vibrations scattering conduction electrons. In an RTD, this resistance change is measured using a Wheatstone bridge or 4-wire ohmmeter and converted to temperature via a calibrated polynomial.

**Sketch (Pt-100 RTD construction):**

```
    ┌─────────────────┐
    │ Protective Sheath │  ← Stainless steel
    │ ┌─────────────┐ │
    │ │ Insulator    │ │  ← MgO powder
    │ │ ┌─────────┐ │ │
    │ │ │ Platinum │ │ │  ← Sensing element
    │ │ │ Wire     │ │ │     (coiled on ceramic bobbin)
    │ │ └─────────┘ │ │
    │ │   Lead Wires │ │  ← 2/3/4-wire configuration
    │ └─────────────┘ │
    └─────────────────┘
          ▲
          │ Sensor tip inserted into thermowell
          ▼
    ┌─────────────────┐
    │ Wheatstone      │
    │ Bridge /        │ → Amplifier → Display
    │ Constant Current│
    └─────────────────┘
```

**Construction:**
- **Platinum wire** (typically 0.05 mm diameter) wound on a **ceramic or glass bobbin**
- **Magnesia (MgO) powder** insulator packed around the wire
- **Stainless steel sheath** for protection
- **Lead wires** brought out as 2-wire, 3-wire, or 4-wire configuration (to compensate for lead resistance)
- The whole assembly is sealed hermetically

**Working:**
1. The RTD is immersed in the process via a thermowell.
2. A constant current (typically 1 mA) is passed through the Pt element.
3. The voltage drop is measured and divided by the current to get the resistance $R_t$.
4. $R_t$ is converted to temperature using the Callendar-Van Dusen equation.

**Callendar-Van Dusen Equation Derivation:**

**Step 1:** For $t \ge 0$ °C (above ice point):
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_t \;=\; R_0\,(1 + A\,t + B\,t^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** For $t < 0$ °C (below ice point), the additional cubic term:
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_t \;=\; R_0\,[\,1 + A\,t + B\,t^2 + C\,(t - 100)\,t^3\,]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — IEC 60751 coefficients for Pt-100:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{°C}^{-1} \\
B &= -5.775 \times 10^{-7}\;\text{°C}^{-2} \\
C &= -4.183 \times 10^{-12}\;\text{°C}^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Linear approximation (for 0 to 100 °C):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
R_t &\;\approx\; R_0\,(1 + \alpha\,t) \\
\alpha &\;=\; 0.00385\;\text{°C}^{-1} \quad \text{(IEC standard for Pt)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Pt-100 specific values:**
- $R_0 = 100\;\Omega$ at 0 °C
- $R_{100} = 138.50\;\Omega$ at 100 °C
- $R_{-200} = 18.52\;\Omega$ at -200 °C

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Temperature Measurement Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert °C to K** in Stefan-Boltzmann or Wien's law calculations. The T in these laws is **absolute temperature (K)**, not Celsius. This is the *#1 reason* students lose full marks in radiation pyrometer problems.
>
> 2. **Using linear approximation** $R_t = R_0(1+\alpha t)$ when the question asks for a *wide-range* calculation. Examiners expect the full Callendar-Van Dusen equation for ranges beyond 0–100 °C.
>
> 3. **Confusing "thermocouple type" with "thermocouple letter"** — students write "Iron-Constantan" but forget the letter "J". Examiners strictly look for the *type letter*.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the cold-junction compensation** in thermocouple problems. The thermocouple *measures a temperature difference*, not an absolute temperature. Always state this clearly.
>
> 5. **Confusing "monochromatic" with "total" pyrometer** — students mix up Wien's displacement law (peak wavelength) with Stefan-Boltzmann (total power). Use the former for *optical* pyrometers, the latter for *total radiation* pyrometers.
>
> 6. **Missing the emissivity term** in radiation calculations. The full form is $q = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$, **not** $q = \sigma T^4$. A blackbody has $\varepsilon = 1$ — students often write $\varepsilon = 1$ when it should be explicitly *given* in the problem.
>
> 7. **Not stating units** in the final answer. The KTU evaluator deducts marks for missing units like K, °C, Ω, mV, W/m².

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print This Before the Exam):**

- [ ] **Temperature scales** — remember the ladder analogy; conversion formula $\frac{C}{5} = \frac{F-32}{9} = \frac{K-273.15}{5}$
- [ ] **Absolute zero** = 0 K = -273.15 °C = -459.67 °F (memorize all three)
- [ ] **Mercury-in-glass** — apparent expansion principle; range **-38 to +357 °C**; toxic
- [ ] **Bimetallic** — two metals with *different* $\alpha$; Invar (low) + Brass/Monel (high); helical coil
- [ ] **RTD (Pt-100)** — most common industrial RTD; $R_0 = 100\;\Omega$; $\alpha = 0.00385$ /°C
- [ ] **Callendar-Van Dusen** — full form for $t<0$ °C includes cubic term; IEC 60751 coefficients are A, B, C
- [ ] **Thermistor** — NTC has resistance *decreasing* with T; sensitivity ~10× higher than RTD; range narrow (-50 to 150 °C)
- [ ] **Thermistor B-equation** — $R_T = R_0 \exp[\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)]$; $\beta \approx 3000{-}5000$ K
- [ ] **Seebeck effect** — EMF at junction of two dissimilar metals; depends on $\Delta T$
- [ ] **Thermocouple types** — J, K, T, E, R, S, B, N — memorize the materials and ranges
- [ ] **Type K** = most common general-purpose; Chromel + Alumel; range -270 to 1260 °C
- [ ] **Thermocouple laws** — Law of Intermediate Metals, Law of Intermediate Temperatures
- [ ] **Cold-junction compensation** — required for accurate thermocouple reading
- [ ] **Optical pyrometer** — uses *visible/monochromatic* radiation; Wien's approximation; range ~700 to 3000 °C
- [ ] **Disappearing filament** — match filament brightness to target; red filter at ~0.65 μm
- [ ] **Radiation pyrometer** — uses *all* wavelengths; Stefan-Boltzmann: $q = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$
- [ ] **Stefan-Boltzmann constant** $\sigma = 5.6703 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴ (memorize)
- [ ] **Wien's displacement law** — $\lambda_{max} T = 2898$ μm·K (memorize)
- [ ] **Emissivity** — 1 for blackbody, <1 for real surfaces; must be accounted for in radiation pyrometers
- [ ] **Lead wire compensation** in RTDs — 3-wire or 4-wire configuration

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Memory Aid:**
> *Contact = touch* (RTD, TC, Mercury); *Non-contact = light* (Optical, Radiation); *TC measures difference, RTD measures absolute; Optical uses one colour, Radiation uses all colours.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ranges of Different Types of Temperature Measuring Instruments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT414 - PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Ranges of Different Types of Temperature Measuring Instruments # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition **Temperature Measuring Instruments** are devices designed to quantify the thermal state of a process or substance by converting thermal energy into a measurable, reproducible signal. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (PECHT414 – Process Instrumentation, Module 2), these instruments are classified based on their **operating principle**, **contact mode** (contact vs. non-contact), and **measurement range**. The **range** of a temperature instrument defines the lower and upper temperature limits between which the device produces a **linear, repeatable, and calibrated output signal** with acceptable accuracy. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must be able to identify, compare, and select temperature measuring instruments based on their **range**, **accuracy**, and **process compatibility**. ## 1.2 Classification of Temperature Measuring Instruments Temperature instruments are broadly classified into two families: ### A. Contact-Type Instruments These instruments physically touch the process medium to detect temperature via conduction or convection. ### B. Non-Contact-Type Instruments These instruments sense temperature by detecting thermal radiation emitted from the target — used for very high temperatures or moving/molten objects. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Thermometer Family"** > Imagine a family of measuring tools where each member is specialized for a specific climate: > - A **mercury thermometer** is like a *bathroom scale* — perfect for ordinary room and body temperatures. > - A **thermocouple** is like a *rugged campfire stick* — built to survive extreme heat. > - A **radiation pyrometer** is like *night-vision goggles* — it doesn't touch the flame; it senses its glow from a distance. > > Each tool has its "comfort zone" — that comfort zone is its **operating range**. ## 1.4 Standard Reference Scales The **International Temperature Scale of 1990 (ITS-90)** is the global standard for calibrating temperature instruments. Three primary reference points are: - **Triple Point of Water:** **0.01 °C (273.16 K)** - **Boiling Point of Water at 1 atm:** **100 °C (373.15 K)** - **Freezing Point of Zinc:** **419.527 °C (692.677 K)** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Range Analysis of Each Instrument ### 2.1.1 Liquid-in-Glass Thermometers - **Principle:** Thermal expansion of a confined liquid (mercury, alcohol, pentane). - **Range:** **−200 °C to +600 °C** - **Sub-Range Breakdown:** - Mercury-filled: −39 °C to 600 °C - Alcohol-filled: −80 °C to 110 °C - Pentane-filled: −200 °C to 30 °C - **Engineering Utility:** Laboratory thermometry, HVAC, low-cost field use. ### 2.1.2 Bimetallic Thermometers - **Principle:** Differential thermal expansion between two bonded metals. - **Range:** **−50 °C to +500 °C** - **Engineering Utility:** Industrial dial gauges, oven thermometers, on/off control switches. ### 2.1.3 Pressure Spring Thermometers (Filled Systems) - **Principle:** Thermal expansion of a fluid (gas, liquid, vapor) creates pressure that deflects a Bourdon tube. - **Range:** **−200 °C to +600 °C** - **Engineering Utility:** Hazardous-area industrial installations, remote transmission. ### 2.1.4 Thermocouples (Seebeck Effect) - **Principle:** Two dissimilar metals joined at a junction generate an EMF proportional to temperature difference. **Seebeck Equation:** $$ E = \int_{T_{\text{ref}}}^{T_{\text{hot}}} \left[ S_B(T) - S_A(T) \right] \, dT $$ Where $S_A(T)$ and $S_B(T)$ are the **Seebeck coefficients** (µV/°C) of the two thermoelements. - **Engineering Utility:** Furnaces, engines, kilns, reactors. ### 2.1.5 Resistance Temperature Detectors (RTDs) - **Principle:** The electrical resistance of a pure metal (typically platinum) varies predictably with temperature. **Callendar-Van Dusen Equation (for industrial Pt100 RTD, −200 °C ≤ T ≤ 0 °C):** $$ R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + AT + BT^2 + CT^3 \left( T - 100 \right) \right] $$ **Callendar-Van Dusen Equation (for industrial Pt100 RTD, 0 °C ≤ T ≤ 850 °C):** $$ R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + AT + BT^2 \right] $$ Where: - $R_0$ = resistance at 0 °C (typically **100 Ω** for Pt100) - $A$ = **3.9083 × 10⁻³ °C⁻¹** - $B$ = **−5.775 × 10⁻⁷ °C⁻²** - $C$ = **−4.183 × 10⁻¹² °C⁻⁴** - **Range:** **−200 °C to +650 °C** - **Engineering Utility:** High-accuracy laboratory and process industry measurements. ### 2.1.6 Thermistors (Thermal Resistors) - **Principle:** Semiconductor material exhibits a strong, nonlinear resistance–temperature relationship. **Steinhart-Hart Equation:** $$ \frac{1}{T} = a + b \ln(R) + c \left[ \ln(R) \right]^3 $$ Where $T$ is in **Kelvin**, $R$ is in **ohms**, and $a$, $b$, $c$ are calibration constants. - **Range:** **−100 °C to +300 °C** - **Engineering Utility:** Biomedical, precision HVAC, electronics thermal protection. ### 2.1.7 Radiation Pyrometers - **Principle:** Detects total thermal radiation emitted by a hot body. **Stefan-Boltzmann Radiation Law:** $$ Q = \varepsilon \sigma T^4 $$ Where: - $Q$ = radiant energy per unit area (W/m²) - $\varepsilon$ = emissivity (dimensionless, $0 \leq \varepsilon \leq 1$) - $\sigma$ = **Stefan-Boltzmann constant = 5.670 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴** - $T$ = absolute temperature in **Kelvin** - **Range:** **500 °C to 3000 °C** - **Engineering Utility:** Molten metals, boilers, kilns, induction heating. ### 2.1.8 Optical Pyrometers - **Principle:** Compares the brightness of the target with an internal calibrated filament. **Planck's Law (Spectral Radiance):** $$ E_{\lambda} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^5 \left( e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1 \right)} $$ Where: - $C_1$ = **3.7418 × 10⁻¹⁶ W·m²** - $C_2$ = **1.4388 × 10⁻² m·K** - $\lambda$ = wavelength (m) - **Range:** **700 °C to 3500 °C** (and beyond) - **Engineering Utility:** Steel plants, foundries, lamp filament calibration. ### 2.1.9 Semiconductor (IC) Temperature Sensors - **Principle:** Exploits the temperature dependence of base-emitter voltage $V_{BE}$ of a transistor pair. - **Range:** **−55 °C to +150 °C** - **Engineering Utility:** Embedded systems, CPU monitoring, portable devices. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference > [!NOTE] > **Master Reference Table — Exam Cheat Sheet** | Instrument | Operating Principle | Typical Range | Output Form | Governing Equation | |---|---|---|---|---| | Liquid-in-Glass | Thermal expansion of liquid | −200 °C to 600 °C | Visual (length) | Linear capillary scale | | Bimetallic | Differential expansion | −50 °C to 500 °C | Mechanical deflection | $\Delta L = L \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | | Pressure Spring | Pressure of confined fluid | −200 °C to 600 °C | Pressure / Displacement | Ideal gas law variants | | Thermocouple Type T | Seebeck Effect | −200 °C to 350 °C | EMF (mV) | $E = \int [S_B - S_A]\,dT$ | | Thermocouple Type J | Seebeck Effect | 0 °C to 750 °C | EMF (mV) | $E = \int [S_B - S_A]\,dT$ | | Thermocouple Type K | Seebeck Effect | −200 °C to 1250 °C | EMF (mV) | $E = \int [S_B - S_A]\,dT$ | | Thermocouple Type R/S | Seebeck Effect | 0 °C to 1450 °C | EMF (mV) | $E = \int [S_B - S_A]\,dT$ | | Thermocouple Type B | Seebeck Effect | 0 °C to 1700 °C | EMF (mV) | $E = \int [S_B - S_A]\,dT$ | | RTD (Pt100) | Resistance change | −200 °C to 650 °C | Resistance (Ω) | Callendar-Van Dusen | | Thermistor (NTC) | Semiconductor resistance | −100 °C to 300 °C | Resistance (Ω) | Steinhart-Hart | | Radiation Pyrometer | Thermal radiation | 500 °C to 3000 °C | Voltage / Current | $Q = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$ | | Optical Pyrometer | Brightness comparison | 700 °C to 3500 °C | Visual / Electrical | Planck's Law | | Semiconductor IC | $V_{BE}$ dependence | −55 °C to 150 °C | Voltage (mV/°C) | Bandgap reference | ## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Selection Strategy When selecting a temperature instrument in practice, the engineer must correlate the **process temperature range** with the **instrument range**, ensuring that the operating point lies within the **20%–80% span rule** for optimal accuracy and longevity. Selection must also account for: - **Response time** (thermocouples fastest, thermistors slow) - **Intrinsic safety** (4-wire RTDs preferred in hazardous zones) - **Ambient temperature compensation** - **Sensor-to-transmitter compatibility** (e.g., RTD wiring — 2-wire, 3-wire, or 4-wire lead configurations) # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Callendar-Van Dusen Equation (RTD Resistance vs. Temperature) ### Objective Derive the polynomial relation between the resistance $R_T$ of a Platinum RTD and its temperature $T$ in °C, given $R_0$ at 0 °C. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Start with the fundamental linear approximation.** For small temperature changes, resistance changes linearly: $$ R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha T \right] $$ **Explanation:** Here $\alpha$ is the mean temperature coefficient of resistance. For pure platinum, $\alpha \approx 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}$ °C⁻¹. **Step 2 — Account for quadratic nonlinearity observed in real platinum.** Experimental calibration reveals that pure platinum exhibits a slight nonlinearity (second-order curvature): $$ R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + A T + B T^2 \right] $$ **Explanation:** The coefficients $A$ and $B$ are determined empirically by calibrating against fixed ITS-90 points (triple point of water, freezing point of zinc, etc.). **Step 3 — Introduce the cubic correction for the sub-zero range.** For temperatures below 0 °C, an additional cubic term appears because the freezing curve of platinum is asymmetric about 0 °C: $$ R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + A T + B T^2 + C T^3 (T - 100) \right] $$ **Explanation:** The factor $(T - 100)$ ensures that the cubic term vanishes exactly at $T = 100$ °C, preserving continuity with the positive-range equation. **Step 4 — Substitute the IEC 60751 standard values.** $$ R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + (3.9083 \times 10^{-3}) T - (5.775 \times 10^{-7}) T^2 - (4.183 \times 10^{-12}) T^3 (T - 100) \right] $$ ### Final Result $$ R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + AT + BT^2 + CT^3(T - 100) \right] $$ with: $$ A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \, °C^{-1}, \quad B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7} \, °C^{-2}, \quad C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12} \, °C^{-4} $$ ### Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A Pt100 RTD is used to measure the temperature of a chemical reactor. Its measured resistance is $R_T = 138.50 \, \Omega$. Determine the temperature in °C using the positive-range Callendar-Van Dusen equation. **Given:** $R_0 = 100 \, \Omega$, $R_T = 138.50 \, \Omega$ **Solution Steps:** **Step A:** Substitute into the equation for $T > 0$: $$ 138.50 = 100 \left[ 1 + (3.9083 \times 10^{-3}) T - (5.775 \times 10^{-7}) T^2 \right] $$ **Step B:** Divide both sides by 100: $$ 1.3850 = 1 + (3.9083 \times 10^{-3}) T - (5.775 \times 10^{-7}) T^2 $$ **Step C:** Rearrange to standard quadratic form: $$ (5.775 \times 10^{-7}) T^2 - (3.9083 \times 10^{-3}) T + 0.3850 = 0 $$ **Step D:** Apply the quadratic formula $T = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$: $$ T = \frac{(3.9083 \times 10^{-3}) \pm \sqrt{(3.9083 \times 10^{-3})^2 - 4(5.775 \times 10^{-7})(0.3850)}}{2(5.775 \times 10^{-7})} $$ **Step E:** Evaluate the discriminant term-by-term: $$ (3.9083 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 1.5275 \times 10^{-5} $$ $$ 4 \cdot (5.775 \times 10^{-7}) \cdot 0.3850 = 8.8935 \times 10^{-7} $$ $$ \Delta = 1.5275 \times 10^{-5} - 8.8935 \times 10^{-7} = 1.4386 \times 10^{-5} $$ **Step F:** Take the square root: $$ \sqrt{\Delta} = \sqrt{1.4386 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.7930 \times 10^{-3} $$ **Step G:** Compute the two roots: $$ T = \frac{(3.9083 \times 10^{-3}) - (3.7930 \times 10^{-3})}{1.155 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.153 \times 10^{-4}}{1.155 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 99.83 \, °C $$ The second root (≈ 6670 °C) is non-physical for this RTD and is rejected. ### Final Answer $$ \boxed{T \approx 99.83 \, °C} $$ This matches the expected behavior: a resistance of 138.50 Ω corresponds to a temperature just below the boiling point of water. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thermocouple EMF Using the Law of Intermediate Metals ### Objective Show why thermocouple EMF depends only on the two junctions (measuring and reference) and not on intermediate wiring. **Step 1 — Consider a closed thermoelectric loop composed of three metals A, B, C with junctions at temperatures $T_{\text{hot}}$, $T_{\text{ref}}$, and $T_{\text{int}}$.** The net EMF around the loop is the sum of the contributions from each pair: $$ E_{\text{net}} = E_{AB}(T_{\text{hot}}) + E_{BC}(T_{\text{int}}) + E_{CA}(T_{\text{ref}}) = 0 $$ **Explanation:** This is a direct consequence of the **Law of Intermediate Metals**, which states that the introduction of a third metal into a thermocouple circuit does not alter the net EMF, provided both of its junctions are at the same temperature. **Step 2 — Solve for $E_{AB}$:** $$ E_{AB}(T_{\text{hot}}, T_{\text{ref}}) = - \left[ E_{BC}(T_{\text{int}}, T_{\text{int}}) + E_{CA}(T_{\text{ref}}) \right] $$ **Explanation:** Since the intermediate metal's two junctions are at identical temperature $T_{\text{int}}$, its contribution cancels. **Step 3 — Apply the Law of Intermediate Temperatures:** $$ E_{AB}(T_{\text{hot}}, T_{\text{ref}}) = E_{AB}(T_{\text{hot}}, T_{\text{int}}) + E_{AB}(T_{\text{int}}, T_{\text{ref}}) $$ **Explanation:** This allows laboratory calibration at a convenient reference temperature (e.g., 0 °C using ice bath) to be extended to any desired reference junction. ### Conclusion The thermocouple EMF is a function only of the **two metals A and B** and the **two junction temperatures** $T_{\text{hot}}$ and $T_{\text{ref}}$. $$ \boxed{E_{AB} = f(T_{\text{hot}}, T_{\text{ref}})} $$ --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Selection Decision Table > [!NOTE] > **Decision Matrix for Instrument Selection Based on Range** | Process Temp Range | Recommended Primary Instrument | Recommended Backup | |---|---|---| | −200 °C to −100 °C | Type T Thermocouple / Pt100 RTD | Thermistor | | −100 °C to 0 °C | Pt100 RTD / Type T Thermocouple | Thermistor | | 0 °C to 300 °C | Thermistor / Type J Thermocouple | Bimetallic | | 300 °C to 600 °C | Type J or Type K Thermocouple | Pt100 RTD | | 600 °C to 1100 °C | Type K Thermocouple | Bimetallic (limited) | | 1100 °C to 1450 °C | Type R/S Thermocouple | — | | 1450 °C to 1700 °C | Type B Thermocouple | — | | Above 1700 °C | Optical Pyrometer / Radiation Pyrometer | — | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Temperature Instrument Range Spectrum (Functional Block Architecture) ```mermaid graph TD subgraph LOW["Low Temperature Range (-200 to 0 C)"] LG["Liquid-in-Glass Thermometer"] TH["Thermistor NTC"] TC_T["Type T Thermocouple"] RTD["Pt100 RTD"] end subgraph MID["Medium Temperature Range (0 to 600 C)"] BM["Bimetallic Thermometer"] PS["Pressure Spring Thermometer"] TC_J["Type J Thermocouple"] TC_K["Type K Thermocouple"] SC["Semiconductor IC Sensor"] end subgraph HIGH["High Temperature Range (600 to 1700 C)"] TC_RS["Type R and S Thermocouple"] TC_B["Type B Thermocouple"] end subgraph ULTRA["Ultra High Temperature Range (Above 1700 C)"] RP["Radiation Pyrometer"] OP["Optical Pyrometer"] end LG --> TH TH --> TC_T TC_T --> RTD RTD --> BM BM --> PS PS --> TC_J TC_J --> TC_K TC_K --> SC SC --> TC_RS TC_RS --> TC_B TC_B --> RP RP --> OP %% Styling for clarity style LOW fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px style MID fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px style HIGH fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px style ULTRA fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Temperature Measurement System Architecture ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["PROCESS MEDIUM
Temperature Source"] --> nodeB["SENSOR ELEMENT
Thermocouple RTD Thermistor Pyrometer"] nodeB --> nodeC["TRANSMITTER
Signal Conditioning and Linearization"] nodeC --> nodeD["COLD JUNCTION COMPENSATION
Reference Compensation"] nodeC --> nodeE["SIGNAL TRANSMISSION
4-20 mA or mV"] nodeE --> nodeF["INDICATOR OR CONTROLLER
Display and Control"] nodeD --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG["OUTPUT
Process Variable PV in deg C"] %% Styling style nodeA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px style nodeB fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900,stroke-width:2px style nodeC fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px style nodeD fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#6600cc,stroke-width:2px style nodeE fill:#ccffe6,stroke:#006633,stroke-width:2px style nodeF fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px style nodeG fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Thermocouple Range Comparison Architecture ```mermaid graph TD subgraph TC_TYPES["THERMOCOUPLE TYPE RANGE MAP"] tct["Type T
-200 to 350 C"] tcj["Type J
0 to 750 C"] tce["Type E
-200 to 900 C"] tck["Type K
-200 to 1250 C"] tcr["Type R
0 to 1450 C"] tcs["Type S
0 to 1450 C"] tcb["Type B
0 to 1700 C"] end tct --> tce tce --> tck tck --> tcr tcr --> tcs tcs --> tcb style TC_TYPES fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#333333,stroke-width:2px style tct fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:1px style tcj fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:1px style tce fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:1px style tck fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:1px style tcr fill:#ff9966,stroke:#cc3300,stroke-width:1px style tcs fill:#ff6633,stroke:#cc3300,stroke-width:1px style tcb fill:#ff3300,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:1px ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *List the temperature ranges over which the following are used:* *(a) Mercury-in-glass thermometer* *(b) Platinum resistance thermometer (Pt100)* *(c) Type K thermocouple* ### Model Answer **(a) Mercury-in-glass thermometer:** **−39 °C to +600 °C** > [!NOTE] > Mercury freezes at −38.83 °C and boils at 356.7 °C, but under pressurized nitrogen inert gas filling, the upper limit extends to 600 °C. **(b) Pt100 RTD:** **−200 °C to +650 °C** > [!IMPORTANT] > The IEC 60751 standard defines Pt100 operation within this range, governed by the Callendar-Van Dusen equation. **(c) Type K Thermocouple:** **−200 °C to +1250 °C** > [!NOTE] > Type K (Chromel–Alumel) is the most widely used industrial thermocouple due to its broad range, low cost, and good oxidation resistance. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Compare the ranges of radiation pyrometers and optical pyrometers. State one application for each.* ### Model Answer | Parameter | Radiation Pyrometer | Optical Pyrometer | |---|---|---| | **Typical Range** | 500 °C to 3000 °C | 700 °C to 3500 °C | | **Principle** | Total radiation emitted | Brightness comparison (mono-chromatic) | | **Output** | Electrical signal | Visual / electrical | | **Application** | Boiler furnace temperature | Steel molten metal temperature | > [!IMPORTANT] > Optical pyrometers extend higher because they rely on a narrow visible wavelength band where blackbody emission is most intense. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Pattern:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### Question A — Module 2 (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 — Model Question]** **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working of a **radiation pyrometer**. State its range and one industrial application. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **ranges and applications** of **Type R, Type S, and Type B thermocouples**. Why are these preferred over Type K in high-temperature zones? **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution — Part (a) **Radiation Pyrometer — Working Principle** A radiation pyrometer (also called a **total radiation pyrometer**) measures temperature by capturing the **total thermal radiation** emitted from a hot body, without making physical contact. **Construction:** - A **radiation receiving element** (usually a thermopile of multiple thermocouples in series) - A **lens or mirror system** to focus incoming radiation onto the thermopile - A **protective housing** with a sighting window **Working:** 1. The hot target emits radiation proportional to the fourth power of its absolute temperature (Stefan-Boltzmann law). 2. The lens focuses this radiation onto the thermopile hot junction. 3. The thermopile generates an EMF proportional to the incident radiative flux. 4. The cold junctions are shielded and maintained at a known reference temperature. 5. The output EMF is amplified and converted to a temperature reading. **Governing Equation:** $$ E_{\text{pyro}} = K \cdot \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot T^4 $$ Where $K$ is the instrument constant. **Range:** **500 °C to 3000 °C** **Application:** Continuous temperature monitoring of **rotary kiln shells** in cement plants, **boiler furnace tubes**, and **induction furnace linings**. **Diagram Reference:** (Use the block diagram from Section 4.2, treating the Sensor Element as the radiation pyrometer block.) ### Model Solution — Part (b) **Type R, S, B Thermocouples — Range and Application Comparison** | Property | Type R | Type S | Type B | |---|---|---|---| | **Composition** | Pt-13%Rh / Pt | Pt-10%Rh / Pt | Pt-30%Rh / Pt-6%Rh | | **Range** | 0 °C to 1450 °C | 0 °C to 1450 °C | 0 °C to 1700 °C | | **Max Continuous** | ~1300 °C | ~1300 °C | ~1600 °C | | **EMF at 1000 °C** | ~10.5 mV | ~9.6 mV | ~4.8 mV | | **Application** | Steel industry, lab standards | Lab standards, glass industry | High-temp furnaces, oxygen sensors | **Why These Are Preferred Over Type K in High-Temperature Zones:** 1. **Higher Temperature Capability:** Type K suffers from **green rot** (chromium oxidation) above 1000 °C, while R/S/B are platinum-rhodium alloys with superior oxidation resistance. 2. **Better Stability:** At temperatures above 1100 °C, the EMF drift in Type K is significant (±2–5 °C per month), whereas R/S/B drifts less than ±0.5 °C per month. 3. **Higher Melting Points:** Platinum-rhodium alloys melt above 1700 °C, suitable for ultra-high-temperature applications like **steelmaking converters**, **glass tank regenerators**, and **gas turbine combustion chambers**. 4. **Reference Standard:** Type S is officially used as the **interpolating instrument** for the ITS-90 scale between 660 °C and 1064 °C (freezing point of silver). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse **"range"** with **"accuracy class"**. While Type K has a broader range, its accuracy degrades sharply above 1000 °C. Always clarify the *operating temperature* vs. *usable range* distinction. --- ### Question B — Module 2 Alternative (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Model Alternative]** **(a)** Explain the construction, working, and range of a **Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD)** with a neat diagram. Derive the Callendar-Van Dusen equation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the temperature measurement ranges of **thermistors, semiconductor IC sensors, and bimetallic thermometers**. When would you prefer each in a process plant? **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution — Part (a) **RTD — Construction, Working, and Range** **Construction:** - A **pure platinum wire** (or thin film) wound on a ceramic or glass mandrel - Encased in a protective stainless-steel sheath - Lead wires brought out as 2-wire, 3-wire, or 4-wire configuration - The element is sometimes embedded in a **wire-wound** or **thin-film** form **Working Principle:** The electrical resistance of pure platinum increases predictably with temperature. As temperature rises, lattice vibrations increase electron scattering, raising resistance. **Standard Designation:** **Pt100** means $R_0 = 100 \, \Omega$ at 0 °C. **Range:** **−200 °C to +650 °C** **Callendar-Van Dusen Equation — Derivation Outline:** Starting from linear approximation $R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T)$ and adding successive corrections for experimentally observed curvature: $$ R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + AT + BT^2 + CT^3(T - 100) \right] $$ The complete derivation is presented in Section 3.1 above. The numerical example showing $T \approx 99.83$ °C for $R_T = 138.50 \, \Omega$ demonstrates practical usage. ### Model Solution — Part (b) **Comparative Range Analysis** | Property | Thermistor | Semiconductor IC | Bimetallic | |---|---|---|---| | **Range** | −100 °C to 300 °C | −55 °C to 150 °C | −50 °C to 500 °C | | **Sensitivity** | Very high (−4%/°C) | Moderate (10 mV/°C) | Low (mechanical) | | **Output** | Resistance | Voltage | Mechanical deflection | | **Accuracy** | ±0.1 °C to ±1 °C | ±1 °C to ±3 °C | ±2 °C to ±5 °C | | **Linearity** | Nonlinear (log) | Excellent | Fairly linear | **Process Plant Selection Guidelines:** - **Choose Thermistor:** When high sensitivity is required in a narrow range — e.g., **freeze protection of pipelines** (around −20 °C), **biomedical incubators**, or **3D printer bed temperature**. - **Choose Semiconductor IC:** When integration with digital electronics is required — e.g., **CPU thermal monitoring**, **battery management systems**, or **HVAC zone control**. - **Choose Bimetallic:** When a rugged, no-power, on/off mechanical signal is needed — e.g., **thermostat switches in ovens**, **thermal cutoff for transformers**, or **fire alarm triggers**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Do not confuse the **operating range** of a sensor with its **accuracy range**. A thermistor's nominal range may be −100 °C to 300 °C, but its calibrated accuracy band is often narrower (e.g., 0 °C to 100 °C). Always cite the **calibrated accuracy range** in industrial applications. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Top Reasons KTU Students Lose Marks on Range Questions** > 1. **Conflating types:** Writing "Type K range is 0–1300 °C" instead of **−200 °C to +1250 °C** — KTU expects the full IEC standard range. > 2. **Forgetting units:** Writing "Range: 100 to 600" without °C loses 1 mark minimum. > 3. **Ignoring pressure effects:** Liquid-in-glass and pressure-spring thermometer ranges depend on fill fluid and inert gas pressure. > 4. **Mixing accuracy and range:** A sensor's range and its accuracy specification are different. Citing them interchangeably costs marks. > 5. **Neglecting ITS-90:** KTU specifically tests knowledge of international temperature standards. Always reference ITS-90 when discussing calibration. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Temperature Instrument Ranges** - ✅ **Liquid-in-Glass:** **−200 °C to 600 °C** — depends on fill fluid (Hg, alcohol, pentane) - ✅ **Bimetallic:** **−50 °C to 500 °C** — based on differential expansion of bonded metals - ✅ **Pressure Spring:** **−200 °C to 600 °C** — pressure of fluid drives Bourdon tube - ✅ **Type T Thermocouple:** **−200 °C to 350 °C** — copper/constantan - ✅ **Type J Thermocouple:** **0 °C to 750 °C** — iron/constantan - ✅ **Type K Thermocouple:** **−200 °C to 1250 °C** — chromel/alumel (industrial workhorse) - ✅ **Type R/S Thermocouple:** **0 °C to 1450 °C** — Pt-Rh / Pt (high-temp standards) - ✅ **Type B Thermocouple:** **0 °C to 1700 °C** — Pt-30%Rh / Pt-6%Rh (highest range) - ✅ **Pt100 RTD:** **−200 °C to 650 °C** — governed by Callendar-Van Dusen equation - ✅ **Thermistor (NTC):** **−100 °C to 300 °C** — high sensitivity, nonlinear (Steinhart-Hart) - ✅ **Radiation Pyrometer:** **500 °C to 3000 °C** — Stefan-Boltzmann law - ✅ **Optical Pyrometer:** **700 °C to 3500 °C** — Planck's law / brightness matching - ✅ **Semiconductor IC:** **−55 °C to 150 °C** — bandgap reference principle **Universal Constants to Memorize:** - Stefan-Boltzmann constant $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴) - Planck constant terms $C_1 = 3.7418 \times 10^{-16}$ W·m², $C_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}$ m·K - Pt100 standard coefficients: $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}$, $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}$, $C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12}$ **Selection Heuristic (20–80% Span Rule):** Always operate the instrument within 20% to 80% of its full range for optimal accuracy and longevity. **International Standard Reference:** All KTU temperature questions implicitly reference the **ITS-90 scale** — be prepared to mention the **triple point of water (0.01 °C / 273.16 K)** as a foundational calibration point.

topics.title: Sources of Errors and Precautions to be Taken in Temperature Measurements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT414 - PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sources of Errors and Precautions to be Taken in Temperature Measurements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Sources of Errors and Precautions in Temperature Measurements

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> In Process Instrumentation, **temperature measurement** is never truly "absolute." Every primary sensor — be it a thermocouple (TC), Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD), thermistor, or filled-system thermometer — introduces *systematic* and *random* errors that deviate the indicated temperature from the **true thermodynamic temperature**. These deviations arise from physical, electrical, chemical, and installation-related phenomena collectively termed **Sources of Error**, and each demands a deliberate engineering **Precaution** to mitigate.

### 1.1 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are measuring the temperature of a cup of hot tea with two different ordinary glass thermometers:

- One thermometer was lying on a **cold windowsill** for an hour before you dipped it in.
- The other was dipped but only its **tip** is in the tea, while most of its body sticks out into the cool air.

Both will read *something*, but neither will show the **true** tea temperature. The first suffers a **lag/hysteresis error**; the second suffers a **stem-conduction (immersion) error**. This is exactly the kind of deviation we discuss as *Sources of Errors* in industrial temperature measurement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (KTU Standard):**
> - **True Temperature (T_true):** Actual thermodynamic temperature of the process medium.
> - **Indicated Temperature (T_ind):** Reading displayed by the sensor-instrument chain.
> - **Error (e):** $e = T_{ind} - T_{true}$, expressed in **°C** or **K**.
> - **Inaccuracy:** Maximum expected error under reference conditions (typically ±1°C or ±0.1% of span).
> - **Repeatability:** Ability to reproduce the same reading under identical conditions (high repeatability ≠ high accuracy).

### 1.2 Why Error Analysis is Critical in KTU Context

In KTU's Outcome-Based Education framework, **Course Outcome CO2** (Apply sensor principles for measurement of process variables) explicitly requires students to:

1. Identify the source of error.
2. Quantify its magnitude.
3. Apply a suitable compensation/precaution technique.

This forms a 3-mark short-answer question almost every KTU University Exam cycle.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Error Sources

Temperature measurement errors are systematically classified into **six** broad categories that the KTU examiner frequently asks:

| S.No | Category | Primary Sensor Affected | Typical Magnitude |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | **Installation / Immersion Errors** | All contact sensors | ±1 °C to ±10 °C |
| 2 | **Lead-Wire / Connection Errors** | RTD, Thermocouple | ±0.5 °C to ±5 °C |
| 3 | **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC) Errors** | Thermocouple only | ±0.5 °C to ±3 °C |
| 4 | **Self-Heating / Sensor Dissipation Errors** | RTD, Thermistor | ±0.1 °C to ±2 °C |
| 5 | **Thermal Shunting & Stem Conduction** | All contact sensors | ±0.5 °C to ±5 °C |
| 6 | **Decalibration / Drift Errors** | All sensors (esp. TC) | ±0.5 °C/year |

---

## 2.2 Detailed Error Breakdown by Sensor Type

### 2.2.1 Thermocouple Errors

**a. Cold Junction Compensation (CJC) Error**
The thermocouple measures the **difference** between hot-junction (T_H) and cold-junction (T_C) temperatures, not absolute T_H. If T_C compensation is imperfect:

$$e_{CJC} = T_{C,actual} - T_{C,compensated}$$

**b. Decalibration / Drift Error**
Caused by:
- High-temperature grain growth in the thermoelements.
- Contamination from the sheath, atmosphere, or insulation (MgO).
- Oxidation, corrosion, or reduction of the wires.
- Cold work hardening.

**c. Thermal Shunting**
Heat conducted *away* from the hot junction through the thermocouple wires themselves, cooling the measurement point:

$$q_{shunt} = -k A \frac{dT}{dx}$$

This is dominant in measuring **small masses** or **low thermal-conductivity surfaces**.

**d. Stray Electromagnetic Interference (EMI)**
Induced EMFs from nearby motors, VFDs, or power cables appear as spurious voltages — an error of millivolts is a serious temperature error.

**e. Lead-Wire Error**
In **Type T / Type K** thermocouples using *extension-grade* wires, mismatch between the wire grade and the thermocouple grade introduces a *junction* EMF.

---

### 2.2.2 RTD (Resistance Temperature Detector) Errors

**a. Lead-Wire Resistance Error**
The RTD measures its own resistance; any resistance in the lead wires adds to the measured value:

$$R_{measured} = R_{RTD} + 2R_{lead} \quad \text{(2-wire configuration)}$$

For a **100 Ω Platinum RTD** with 2-wire leads of total 2 Ω (≈ 10 m of copper wire):

$$e_{lead} \approx 2 \, \Omega \times \frac{1 \, °C}{0.385 \, \Omega} \approx 5.2 \, °C$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Most common KTU mistake:** Students write $R = R_{RTD} + 2R_{lead}$ but forget the factor of **2** for the round-trip path. Examiners deduct 1 mark.

**b. Self-Heating Error**
The measurement current $I$ through the RTD dissipates power $P = I^2 R$, raising the sensor above the process temperature:

$$\Delta T_{self} = \frac{I^2 \cdot R_{RTD}}{S_h} = \frac{P}{S_h}$$

where $S_h$ is the **self-heating coefficient** (W/°C) specified by the manufacturer.

> **Typical values:** $S_h \approx 50 \text{ mW/°C}$ (in still water) → at $I = 1 \text{ mA}$, $P = 0.1 \text{ mW}$ → $\Delta T \approx 0.002 \, °C$.

**c. Stem Conduction / Immersion Error**
Heat conducted along the probe stem from a hot process to a cold environment:

$$q_{stem} = k_{stem} \cdot A_{stem} \cdot \frac{dT}{dx}$$

This "robs" heat from the sensing element.

---

### 2.2.3 Thermistor Errors

- **Self-Heating:** Highly critical since thermistor resistance is high (kΩ range) → even small currents produce large $I^2R$ dissipation.
- **Stability / Drift:** NTC thermistors drift, especially above 150 °C.
- **Limited Range:** Nonlinear Steinhart-Hart relation; extrapolation outside calibrated range produces large errors.

---

### 2.2.4 Filled-System / Bimetallic Errors

- **Capillary & Bulb Errors:** Ambient temperature variations along the capillary cause errors unless compensated.
- **Hysteresis & Friction** in Bourdon tubes.
- **Barometric Compensation** for gas-filled systems.

---

## 2.3 The Immersion-Length Rule (KTU High-Yield)

For accurate measurement, the **immersion length (L)** must satisfy:

$$L \geq 10 \cdot D_{sheath} + \frac{D_{sheath}}{0.4}$$

A simplified practical rule used in industry:

$$L_{min} \approx 15 \, D_{sheath}$$

> **Example:** For a 6 mm probe → $L_{min} \approx 90$ mm immersion. Anything less → stem-conduction error dominates.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Revision Table

| Formula | Meaning | Sensor | Units |
|:---|:---|:---|:---:|
| $R_{measured} = R_{RTD} + 2R_{lead}$ | 2-wire lead error | RTD | Ω |
| $\Delta T_{self} = I^2 R / S_h$ | Self-heating | RTD, Thermistor | °C |
| $e_{CJC} = T_{C,actual} - T_{C,comp}$ | Cold-junction error | TC | °C |
| $q_{shunt} = -k A (dT/dx)$ | Heat loss along wires | TC | W |
| $T_{ind} = T_{true} + e_{installation} + e_{lead} + e_{self} + e_{drift}$ | Total error model | All | °C |
| $L_{min} \approx 15 D_{sheath}$ | Immersion rule | All contact | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** In a refinery reactor where temperature must be controlled to ±2 °C at 850 °C, a single uncompensated cold-junction error of 5 °C could trigger a false catalytic-converter shutdown costing ₹ lakhs per hour. This is why every KTU question on "precautions" tests *both* identification and the corresponding engineering fix.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Lead-Wire Error in 2-Wire RTD

We derive the exact error introduced by lead resistance in a 2-wire RTD measurement.

**Given:**
- Platinum RTD nominal resistance: $R_0 = 100 \, \Omega$ at $0 \, °C$
- Temperature coefficient (European curve, IEC 60751): $\alpha = 0.00385 \, °C^{-1}$
- Each lead wire resistance: $R_{lead} = 1.0 \, \Omega$
- True process temperature: $T_{true} = 100 \, °C$

**Step 1 — True RTD resistance at T_true**

The Callendar-Van Dusen equation (simplified for T > 0 °C, ignoring higher terms):

$$R_{RTD} = R_0 \left( 1 + \alpha \, T \right)$$

Substituting:

$$R_{RTD} = 100 \left( 1 + 0.00385 \times 100 \right)$$

$$R_{RTD} = 100 \left( 1 + 0.385 \right)$$

$$R_{RTD} = 138.5 \, \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Measured resistance (2-wire configuration)**

In a 2-wire RTD, the same two wires carry the excitation current *and* sense the voltage — so the lead resistance is added twice (out and back):

$$R_{measured} = R_{RTD} + 2 R_{lead}$$

$$R_{measured} = 138.5 + 2 \times 1.0$$

$$R_{measured} = 140.5 \, \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Indicated (erroneous) temperature**

Solving the linearized Pt100 equation for T:

$$T_{ind} = \frac{R_{measured} - R_0}{\alpha \, R_0}$$

$$T_{ind} = \frac{140.5 - 100}{0.00385 \times 100}$$

$$T_{ind} = \frac{40.5}{0.385}$$

$$T_{ind} = 105.19 \, °C$$

**Step 4 — Error magnitude**

$$e_{lead} = T_{ind} - T_{true} = 105.19 - 100 = 5.19 \, °C$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Just 2 Ω of total lead-wire resistance produces an error of **>5 °C** at 100 °C. This is why industry uses **3-wire or 4-wire RTDs** for any installation longer than a few meters.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Self-Heating Error in an RTD

**Given:**
- RTD resistance: $R = 138.5 \, \Omega$ at 100 °C
- Excitation current: $I = 5 \text{ mA}$
- Self-heating coefficient in still air: $S_h = 2.5 \text{ mW/°C}$

**Step 1 — Power dissipated in RTD**

$$P = I^2 R = (5 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 138.5$$

$$P = 25 \times 10^{-6} \times 138.5$$

$$P = 3.4625 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{W}$$

$$P \approx 3.46 \, \text{mW}$$

**Step 2 — Temperature rise above ambient**

$$\Delta T_{self} = \frac{P}{S_h} = \frac{3.46 \, \text{mW}}{2.5 \, \text{mW/°C}}$$

$$\Delta T_{self} = 1.385 \, °C$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** A 5 mA excitation — which sounds tiny — raises the RTD by 1.385 °C in still air. Modern transmitters use **pulsed excitation** or **1 mA current** to keep this error below 0.1 °C.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Immersion Error via Thermal Resistance Model

For a cylindrical probe of length L, diameter D, thermal conductivity k, immersed in a fluid of convective heat-transfer coefficient h:

$$T_{tip} - T_{fluid} = \frac{q}{h \, A_{tip}} = \frac{q}{h \cdot \pi D^2 / 4}$$

Where the heat loss along the stem equals:

$$q = k_{stem} \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \frac{T_{tip} - T_{ambient}}{L}$$

Substituting:

$$T_{tip} - T_{fluid} = \frac{k_{stem} (T_{tip} - T_{ambient})}{h \, L}$$

Solving for the error $e_{imm} = T_{tip} - T_{fluid}$:

$$e_{imm} = \frac{k_{stem} \, (T_{tip} - T_{ambient})}{h \, L - k_{stem}}$$

For $h \, L \gg k_{stem}$ (long immersion), this simplifies to:

$$\boxed{e_{imm} \approx \frac{k_{stem}}{h \, L} \left( T_{tip} - T_{ambient} \right)}$$

**Numerical Example:**
- $k_{stem} = 16 \, \text{W/m·K}$ (stainless steel 316)
- $h = 500 \, \text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$ (forced convection gas)
- $L = 0.05 \, \text{m}$ (insufficient immersion)
- $T_{tip} - T_{ambient} = 200 \, °C$

$$e_{imm} \approx \frac{16}{500 \times 0.05} \times 200 = \frac{16}{25} \times 200$$

$$e_{imm} = 0.64 \times 200 = 128 \, °C$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Insight:** Halving the immersion from 50 mm to 25 mm doubles the error. This is why engineers physically check $L_{min} \approx 15 D$ in the field. Examiners love this derivation as a 7-mark Part-B question.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Precautions vs. Error Source

| Error Source | Sensor | Precaution / Compensation Method | Effectiveness |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---:|
| Lead-wire resistance | RTD | Use 3-wire or 4-wire Kelvin connection | ★★★★★ |
| Cold-junction drift | TC | Use electronic CJC chip (e.g., AD595) | ★★★★★ |
| Self-heating | RTD / Thermistor | Pulsed excitation, lower current | ★★★★ |
| Stem conduction | All contact | Increase immersion, use thermowells | ★★★★ |
| Decalibration / drift | TC | Periodic re-calibration, noble-metal TC | ★★★ |
| Thermal shunting | TC | Use small-diameter thermoelements, bead junction | ★★★ |
| EMI / stray pickup | TC | Twisted shielded pairs, grounded sheath | ★★★★ |
| Process corrosion | All | Use protective sheaths, thermowells | ★★★★ |
| Hysteresis (RTD) | RTD | Use strain-free construction | ★★★ |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Error Sources & Compensation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Medium at T_true] --> B[Contact Temperature Sensor]
    B --> C{Temperature Transducer}
    C --> D[Signal Conditioner]
    D --> E[Display / Controller]

    E1[Installation Error] -.->|affects| B
    E2[Lead Wire Error] -.->|affects| C
    E3[Cold Junction Error] -.->|affects| C
    E4[Self Heating Error] -.->|affects| B
    E5[Stem Conduction Error] -.->|affects| B
    E6[Drift and Decalibration] -.->|affects| B
    E7[EMI Stray Pickup] -.->|affects| D

    P1[Use proper immersion L_min 15D] -.->|mitigates| E1
    P2[Use 3 wire or 4 wire RTD] -.->|mitigates| E2
    P3[Electronic CJC with RTD at terminals] -.->|mitigates| E3
    P4[Use low excitation current below 1 mA] -.->|mitigates| E4
    P5[Use thermowells with high thermal conductivity] -.->|mitigates| E5
    P6[Periodic NIST traceable calibration] -.->|mitigates| E6
    P7[Twisted shielded cables with sheath ground] -.->|mitigates| E7
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — 2-Wire vs 3-Wire vs 4-Wire RTD

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TwoWire[2 Wire RTD]
        R1[RTD Element] --- L1a[Lead Resistance RL]
        R1 --- L1b[Lead Resistance RL]
        L1a --- M1[Ohmmeter or Bridge]
        L1b --- M1
    end

    subgraph ThreeWire[3 Wire RTD]
        R2[RTD Element] --- L2a[Lead RL]
        R2 --- L2b[Lead RL]
        R2 --- L2c[Lead RL common]
        L2a --- M2[Bridge or Transmitter]
        L2b --- M2
        L2c --- M2
    end

    subgraph FourWire[4 Wire RTD Kelvin]
        R3[RTD Element] --- L3a[Excite Lead]
        R3 --- L3b[Excite Lead]
        R3 --- L3c[Sense Lead High]
        R3 --- L3d[Sense Lead Low]
        L3a --- M3[Precision Source]
        L3b --- M3
        L3c --- M4[High Impedance Voltmeter]
        L3d --- M4
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Decision Tree — Choosing a Precaution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What is the sensor type?}
    Q1 -->|Thermocouple| TC[Apply CJC and twisted shielded cables]
    Q1 -->|RTD| RTD[Apply 3 wire or 4 wire lead compensation]
    Q1 -->|Thermistor| THM[Limit excitation current and decouple self heating]

    TC --> TC1{Process is high temperature above 1000 C?}
    TC1 -->|Yes| TC2[Use noble metal TC Type R S or B with alumina sheath]
    TC1 -->|No| TC3[Use base metal TC Type K with stainless sheath]

    RTD --> RTD1{Cable run longer than 10 m?}
    RTD1 -->|Yes| RTD2[Use 4 wire Kelvin connection]
    RTD1 -->|No| RTD3[Use 3 wire connection]

    THM --> THM1{Process is above 150 C?}
    THM1 -->|Yes| THM2[Switch to RTD or TC]
    THM1 -->|No| THM3[Use NTC with low excitation below 0.1 mA]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Exam 2024]`
**List any three sources of errors in thermocouple temperature measurement. How is the cold-junction error compensated?**

**Model Answer:**

Three sources of error in thermocouple temperature measurement are:

1. **Cold-junction compensation error:** A thermocouple generates an EMF proportional to the temperature difference between its measuring (hot) and reference (cold) junctions. Any deviation of the reference junction temperature from its compensated value introduces error.
2. **Decalibration / drift error:** Prolonged exposure to high temperatures, oxidizing/reducing atmospheres, or contamination from insulation causes a permanent change in the Seebeck coefficient.
3. **Thermal shunting / stem conduction:** Heat conducted away from the measuring junction along the thermocouple wires cools the junction below the true process temperature. This is critical when measuring small masses or surfaces.

**Cold-junction compensation method:**
A precision temperature sensor (typically a semiconductor IC such as the **AD595** or a calibrated thermistor/RTD) is mounted at the terminal block where the thermocouple wires connect to the copper extension wires. Its output is added (in software or analog circuitry) to the thermocouple EMF so that the indicated temperature corresponds to absolute temperature rather than differential temperature. Modern digital transmitters perform CJC electronically with an accuracy better than **±0.5 °C**.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary 2023]`
**Explain self-heating error in an RTD. How is it minimized?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Self-heating error is the temperature rise of the RTD element above the true process temperature due to $I^2 R$ power dissipation by the measurement (excitation) current flowing through the sensor.

**Quantification:**

$$\Delta T_{self} = \frac{I^2 \, R_{RTD}}{S_h}$$

where $S_h$ (mW/°C) is the manufacturer's specified self-heating coefficient in a defined medium.

**Minimization methods:**

1. Use the **lowest possible excitation current** (typically 1 mA or less for Pt100).
2. Use **pulsed / AC excitation** so that average dissipation is small.
3. Use a **larger sensor body** (higher thermal mass, higher $S_h$).
4. Ensure good **thermal contact** between RTD and the measured medium (high $h$, e.g., use thermowell oil or forced convection).
5. Apply a **digital correction** based on known $S_h$ and measured $I$.

For a 100 Ω Pt100 RTD in still water ($S_h \approx 50$ mW/°C), at $I = 1$ mA:
$P = 0.1$ mW → $\Delta T \approx 0.002$ °C (negligible).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **lead-wire compensation methods** in 2-wire, 3-wire, and 4-wire RTD connections. Derive an expression for the error in the 2-wire method. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Discuss the **sources of errors in thermocouple measurements** and explain the precautions to be taken to minimize them. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**2-Wire Connection:**
The same two leads carry the excitation current and sense the voltage. Both lead resistances ($R_{lead,1}$ and $R_{lead,2}$) add to the RTD resistance:

$$R_{measured} = R_{RTD} + R_{lead,1} + R_{lead,2} = R_{RTD} + 2R_{lead}$$

The corresponding temperature error (using linearized Pt100 equation, $R_{RTD} = R_0(1 + \alpha T)$):

$$e_{2-wire} = \frac{2 R_{lead}}{\alpha \, R_0}$$

**Numerical example:** For Pt100 with $R_0 = 100 \, \Omega$, $\alpha = 0.00385$, total lead resistance = $2 \times 1 \, \Omega$:

$$e_{2-wire} = \frac{2}{0.00385 \times 100} = \frac{2}{0.385} \approx 5.19 \, °C$$

This is unacceptably high, hence 2-wire is used only for short probe assemblies with built-in leads.

**3-Wire Connection:**
A third wire (identical to the other two) is brought to one terminal of the RTD. Two leads are on one side, one on the other. A Wheatstone bridge or ratiometric measurement cancels the lead resistance (assuming all three leads are equal):

$$R_{measured} = R_{RTD} + R_{lead} - R_{lead} = R_{RTD}$$

Leading-error is **theoretically zero** if the leads are perfectly matched.

**4-Wire (Kelvin) Connection:**
Separate pairs of "excite" and "sense" leads. The sense leads carry virtually no current (high-impedance voltmeter), so there is no $IR$ drop in them:

$$V_{sense} = I \cdot R_{RTD}$$

The excitation current $I$ is forced through the excite leads; their resistance does not affect the measurement. This is the **most accurate** method, used in laboratory and high-precision industrial RTDs.

> **Diagram:** Refer to SECTION 4.2 Mermaid schematic.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The **major sources of errors** in thermocouple temperature measurement and their corresponding **precautions** are tabulated below:

| Source of Error | Description | Precaution |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Cold-junction compensation (CJC) error** | TC measures differential EMF, not absolute | Use precision IC CJC (e.g., AD595, MAX31855) at terminal block |
| **Decalibration / drift** | High-T grain growth, oxidation | Periodic re-calibration; noble-metal TC (Pt/Pt-Rh) for >1000 °C |
| **Thermal shunting** | Heat conducted along wires away from junction | Use small-diameter (≤ 0.5 mm) thermoelements; bead/exposed junction |
| **Lead-wire / extension-wire error** | Mismatch between TC grade and extension grade | Use proper extension wires matching TC type; avoid splices |
| **EMI / stray pickup** | Induced EMFs from power cables, VFDs | Twisted shielded pairs; grounded sheath; route away from noise sources |
| **Installation / immersion error** | Stem conduction from hot process to ambient | Immerse at least 15 × sheath diameter; use thermowells |
| **Atmospheric contamination** | Reducing atmospheres corrode iron in Type K | Use protective sheaths; gas-tight assemblies |
| **Reference junction at non-ice temperature** | Old method used 0 °C ice bath | Modern electronic CJC replaces ice bath (more reliable) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students frequently list only 3–4 errors and forget **thermal shunting** or **EMI pickup**. Both carry 1 mark each in the 7-mark question. Always list **at least six** distinct error sources with their remedies.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary 2024]`

**(a)** A platinum RTD with $R_0 = 100 \, \Omega$ and $\alpha = 0.00385 \, °C^{-1}$ is connected with **2-wire** leads of total resistance $1.5 \, \Omega$ per lead. The RTD is at a true temperature of 250 °C. Calculate the **temperature error** introduced by the lead resistance. If a **3-wire** configuration were used (identical leads), what would be the **residual error**? **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Explain **stem-conduction error** (immersion error) in a contact-type temperature sensor. Derive the expression for the error and state the **immersion rule** used in industry. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — True RTD resistance at 250 °C**

$$R_{RTD} = R_0 \left( 1 + \alpha \, T \right)$$

$$R_{RTD} = 100 \left( 1 + 0.00385 \times 250 \right)$$

$$R_{RTD} = 100 \left( 1 + 0.9625 \right) = 196.25 \, \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Measured resistance in 2-wire**

$$R_{measured} = R_{RTD} + 2 R_{lead} = 196.25 + 2 \times 1.5 = 199.25 \, \Omega$$

**Step 3 — Indicated temperature**

$$T_{ind} = \frac{R_{measured} - R_0}{\alpha \, R_0} = \frac{199.25 - 100}{0.00385 \times 100}$$

$$T_{ind} = \frac{99.25}{0.385} = 257.79 \, °C$$

**Step 4 — Error**

$$e_{2-wire} = 257.79 - 250 = 7.79 \, °C$$

**Step 5 — Residual error in 3-wire (with matched leads)**

In a perfectly balanced 3-wire configuration:

$$R_{measured} = R_{RTD} + R_{lead} - R_{lead} = R_{RTD}$$

Therefore the **residual error is zero** (theoretically). In practice, with ±5% lead mismatch, a residual error of ≈ ±0.4 °C remains.

> **Answer:** 2-wire error = **7.79 °C**; 3-wire residual ≈ **0 °C** (theoretical).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Stem-conduction (immersion) error** arises because the metallic sheath and internal wires of a temperature sensor conduct heat from the hot measurement zone (inside the pipe/vessel) to the cold environment (outside, where the transmitter head is mounted). This conduction:

1. **Cools the sensing tip** below the true fluid temperature.
2. Creates a temperature gradient along the probe.

**Derivation (refer SECTION 3.3 for the full derivation):**

$$\boxed{e_{imm} = \frac{k_{stem}}{h \, L - k_{stem}} \left( T_{tip} - T_{ambient} \right)}$$

For long immersion ($hL \gg k_{stem}$):

$$e_{imm} \approx \frac{k_{stem} \, (T_{tip} - T_{ambient})}{h \, L}$$

**Immersion Rule:**
Industry uses the practical rule:

$$L_{min} \approx 15 \, D_{sheath}$$

For a 6 mm probe, $L_{min} \approx 90$ mm. Anything less dramatically increases the error.

**Precautions:**
1. Ensure adequate immersion (≥ 15 D).
2. Insulate the external portion of the probe to reduce $T_{tip} - T_{ambient}$.
3. Use thermowells made of high-conductivity material (stainless steel, Inconel).
4. For surface-temperature measurement, use surface-mount probes with minimal stem.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Part b):** Students commonly write the formula without explaining the **assumptions** (long-immersion limit, constant $h$, no radiation). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if the limits of validity are not stated. Always mention **"for $hL \gg k_{stem}$"**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU:**

- [x] **Six error categories** to remember: Installation, Lead-wire, CJC, Self-heating, Stem conduction, Drift.
- [x] **2-wire RTD** error formula: $e = 2R_{lead} / (\alpha R_0)$. With 2 Ω leads on Pt100 → **≈ 5.2 °C error**.
- [x] **3-wire RTD** cancels lead error *only if leads are perfectly matched*.
- [x] **4-wire (Kelvin) RTD** is the gold standard — used in laboratory and high-precision applications.
- [x] **Self-heating** is critical in thermistors (high R) → use currents < 0.1 mA.
- [x] **Immersion rule:** $L_{min} \approx 15 \times D_{sheath}$.
- [x] **CJC** uses an electronic IC sensor at the terminal block — never rely on the old ice-bath method.
- [x] **Thermal shunting** is dominant for small-mass measurements → use fine-gauge (≤ 0.5 mm) thermocouples.
- [x] **EMI shielding**: always use twisted, shielded extension wires with grounded sheath.
- [x] **Drift**: Type K TC drifts above 1000 °C due to oxidation of the chromel wire — use Type R/S or noble-metal TC.
- [x] **Decalibration** is caused by **grain growth, contamination, oxidation, cold-work hardening** — remember all four.
- [x] **Thermowells** protect sensors but introduce a **time lag**; choose material with high $k$ (Inconel, SS316).
- [x] **Typical self-heating coefficient** $S_h$ for Pt100 in still water ≈ 50 mW/°C; in still air ≈ 2.5 mW/°C.
- [x] **Total error model:** $T_{ind} = T_{true} + \Sigma e_i$ — always *sum* errors with worst-case sign for design margin.
- [x] **KTU 14-mark question pattern**: One sub-part is always a *derivation* (RTD error, immersion error, or self-heating); the other sub-part is *qualitative discussion* of all error sources + precautions.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
