# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**List any three spectroscopic methods of composition analysis and state one advantage and one limitation of each.**

**Model Answer (Structured):**

The three spectroscopic methods are:

1. **UV-Visible Spectrophotometry:**
   - *Advantage:* Non-destructive, highly sensitive (ppm–ppb), rapid analysis.
   - *Limitation:* Susceptible to interferences from particulate matter or co-existing absorbing species.

2. **Infrared (FTIR) Spectroscopy:**
   - *Advantage:* Provides fingerprint identification of organic functional groups; excellent for gas-phase streams.
   - *Limitation:* Water strongly absorbs IR, making aqueous samples difficult.

3. **Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy (AAS):**
   - *Advantage:* Highly sensitive and selective for trace metals (Pb, Cd, Hg at ppb level).
   - *Limitation:* One element at a time; requires hollow-cathode lamp for each element.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series II]
**Explain the principle of conductometric titration with a suitable graph.**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle:** Conductometric titration is a volumetric technique in which the **electrical conductivity** of the solution is continuously monitored as titrant is added. As one electrolyte is progressively replaced by another, the overall ion concentration changes, producing a **measurable change in conductance**. The **equivalence point** is identified as a sharp **break in the linear plot** of conductance vs. titrant volume.

**Example — Titration of strong acid (HCl) with strong base (NaOH):**
- Initially, H⁺ ions (very high mobility) dominate.
- As NaOH is added, H⁺ is neutralised: H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O.
- Conductance **decreases sharply** until equivalence.
- Beyond equivalence, excess OH⁻ ions increase conductance.

```
Conductance (mS/cm)
   |
   |              /
   |             /
   |____________/   <-- Equivalence Point (V_eq)
   |           /
   |          /
   |_________/_______________ Volume of NaOH (mL)
```

**Key mark-bearing points:**
- Plot conductance on Y-axis, titrant volume on X-axis.
- Identify equivalence point as intersection of two linear segments.
- Mention that **dilution correction** must be applied (multiply by $(V + v)/V$).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Internal Choice Structure (As per KTU ESE Pattern):**
> Answer **ONE** of the following. Each carries **14 marks** with sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### Question A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified Module-4 Pattern]
**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a UV-Visible Spectrophotometer for composition analysis. Derive the Beer-Lambert Law.  (7 Marks)**

**(b) List the major spectroscopic methods with their principle, application, and one limitation each. Compare spectroscopy with chromatography.  (7 Marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a):

**Working Principle:**
A UV-Vis spectrophotometer measures the **absorption of ultraviolet or visible light** by a sample to determine its composition. The amount of light absorbed is proportional to the concentration of the analyte, following the **Beer-Lambert Law**.

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Light Source] → [Monochromator] → [Beam Splitter] → [Sample Cell]
                                                          ↓
                                                    [Detector]
                                                          ↓
                                                   [Amplifier]
                                                          ↓
                                                   [Display]
```

**Working Steps:**

1. **Light Source:** A deuterium lamp (190–380 nm for UV) or tungsten-halogen lamp (380–800 nm for visible) emits a continuous spectrum.
2. **Monochromator:** A prism or grating isolates a narrow band of wavelengths.
3. **Beam Splitter:** Divides the beam into a reference path (air/blank) and a sample path.
4. **Sample Cell:** A cuvette (typically 1 cm path length) holds the absorbing solution.
5. **Detector:** Photomultiplier tube or photodiode measures transmitted intensity.
6. **Readout:** Microprocessor computes absorbance $A = -\log_{10}(I/I_0)$.

**Derivation of Beer-Lambert Law:**

**Step 1 — Differential Equation:**
For a thin slice of thickness $\mathrm{d}x$ at intensity $I$:

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}I}{I} = -\mu \, \mathrm{d}x$$

**Step 2 — Integrate:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\mu \, b$$

**Step 3 — Convert to base-10 log by substituting $\mu = 2.303 \, \varepsilon \, c$:**

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \varepsilon \, c \, b$$

**Step 4 — Identify absorbance:**

$$\boxed{A = \varepsilon \, c \, b}$$

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b):

**Major Spectroscopic Methods:**

| Method | Principle | Application | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| UV-Vis | Absorption of UV/Vis light | Organics, metal complexes | Subject to interferences |
| IR (FTIR) | Vibrational transitions | Functional group ID | Aqueous samples difficult |
| NMR | Nuclear spin resonance | Molecular structure | Expensive, lab-only |
| AAS | Atomic vapor absorption | Trace metals | Single element per run |
| XRF | X-ray emission | Elemental composition | Matrix effects |

**Comparison — Spectroscopy vs. Chromatography:**

| Parameter | Spectroscopy | Chromatography |
|---|---|---|
| Selectivity | Moderate to high | Very high |
| Speed | Fast (seconds) | Slow (minutes to hours) |
| Multi-component | Possible with multichannel detectors | Excellent (separation first) |
| Continuous on-line | Yes | Difficult |
| Cost | High | Very high |
| Skill required | Moderate | High |

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified Pattern]
**(a) Explain the principle and working of Gas Chromatography with a block diagram. Define the terms: Retention Time, Adjusted Retention Time, and Resolution.  (7 Marks)**

**(b) Discuss the principle of conductivity measurement. Derive the relationship between conductance, cell constant, and conductivity. Explain the working of an electrodeless (toroidal) conductivity analyzer with a diagram.  (7 Marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a):

**Principle of Gas Chromatography:**
GC separates volatile components of a mixture by carrying them in a **mobile gas phase** (He, N₂, H₂) through a **stationary liquid phase** coated on an inert solid. Components partition between the two phases based on their **volatility and solubility**, eluting at different times.

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Carrier Gas] → [Pressure Regulator] → [Injector]
                                            ↓
                                    [Oven with Column]
                                            ↓
                                       [Detector] → [Recorder]
```

**Working Steps:**

1. Carrier gas flows at controlled flow rate.
2. Micro-syringe injects sample through septum into heated injector.
3. Vaporized sample is swept onto the column (in temperature-programmed oven).
4. Components separate as they travel.
5. Detector (FID/TCD) generates signal proportional to component concentration.
6. Recorder produces chromatogram with peaks.

**Key Definitions:**

- **Retention Time ($t_R$):** Time from sample injection to the appearance of the peak maximum.
- **Adjusted Retention Time ($t_R'$):** $t_R' = t_R - t_M$, where $t_M$ is the dead time (time for unretained species).
- **Resolution ($R_s$):** A measure of how well two adjacent peaks are separated:

$$R_s = \frac{2(t_{R2} - t_{R1})}{w_1 + w_2}$$

For **baseline separation**, $R_s \geq 1.5$.

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b):

**Principle of Conductivity Measurement:**
Electrical conductivity measures the ability of a solution to conduct current due to the presence of **mobile ions**. Conductivity is proportional to ion concentration, charge, and mobility.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Conductance:**

$$G = \frac{1}{R} \quad [\text{S, siemens}]$$

**Step 2 — Resistance of electrolyte column:**

$$R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

where $\rho$ = resistivity, $L$ = electrode spacing, $A$ = electrode area.

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$G = \frac{A}{\rho \, L}$$

**Step 4 — Conductivity $\kappa = 1/\rho$:**

$$G = \kappa \cdot \frac{A}{L}$$

**Step 5 — Cell constant $K_{\text{cell}} = L/A$:**

$$\boxed{\kappa = G \cdot K_{\text{cell}}}$$

**Electrodeless (Toroidal) Conductivity Analyzer:**

This design uses **two toroidal coils** encapsulated in a polymer housing — no electrodes contact the process fluid. This makes it ideal for **corrosive, abrasive, or fouling** streams.

**Working Principle:**

1. **Drive Coil:** Energized with AC signal, creates alternating magnetic field.
2. **Sense Coil:** Located on opposite side of the flow-through cell. Magnetic field induces an EMF proportional to solution conductivity.
3. **Signal Processing:** Rectified and linearized signal is temperature-compensated (to 25 °C reference).
4. **Output:** 4–20 mA proportional to conductivity.

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Process Flow] → [Toroidal Sensor] → [AC Drive Coil]
                                              ↓
                                       [Sense Coil EMF]
                                              ↓
                                    [Rectifier + Amplifier]
                                              ↓
                                  [Temp Compensation 25°C]
                                              ↓
                                      [Linearized Output]
```

**Advantages over contacting type:**
- Immune to electrode polarization.
- Resistant to fouling and coating.
- Suitable for highly conductive (> 100 mS/cm) and aggressive chemicals.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Composition Analysis Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing $A$ and $T$:** Students often write $A = \log(I/I_0)$ instead of $A = \log(I_0/I)$ — losing **1 mark** instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting dilution correction in conductometric titration:** Conductance must be multiplied by $(V + v)/V$ before plotting — examiners specifically look for this in **CO4 questions**.
> 3. **Mixing units:** Reporting $N$ as "plates per cm" or HETP as "plates" instead of cm — both carry unit-mark penalties.
> 4. **Missing $\varepsilon$ (molar absorptivity) in Beer-Lambert:** Many students skip defining the variables — full marks require **all three** ($\varepsilon$, $b$, $c$) defined with units.
> 5. **Writing "GC measures composition directly":** GC **separates** first, then **detects** — emphasize the two-stage process.
> 6. **Ignoring temperature compensation in conductivity:** All conductivity values are reported **normalized to 25 °C** unless stated otherwise.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print this out!)**

### 🔬 Spectroscopic Methods
- ✅ Based on **EM radiation–matter interaction** (absorption, emission, scattering).
- ✅ Governed by **Beer-Lambert Law:** $A = \varepsilon b c$.
- ✅ Fast response, multi-component capability, non-destructive.
- ✅ Includes UV-Vis, IR, NMR, AAS, XRF.
- ✅ Key drawback: spectral interferences; particulate sensitivity.

### 🧪 Chromatography Methods
- ✅ Two phases: **mobile** (gas/liquid) + **stationary** (solid/liquid film).
- ✅ Separates mixtures based on **differential partitioning**.
- ✅ Core equations: $k = (t_R - t_M)/t_M$, $N = 16(t_R/w)^2$, $R_s = 2(t_{R2} - t_{R1})/(w_1 + w_2)$.
- ✅ $R_s \geq 1.5$ for **baseline separation**.
- ✅ Includes GC, HPLC, Ion Chromatography.
- ✅ Key drawback: slow, requires skilled operator, not real-time continuous.

### ⚡ Conductivity Methods
- ✅ Measures **ionic content** via electrical conductance.
- ✅ Core equation: $\kappa = G \cdot K_{\text{cell}}$.
- ✅ **Cell constant** $K_{\text{cell}} = L/A$ — geometry-dependent.
- ✅ Includes contacting, electrodeless (toroidal), conductometric titration.
- ✅ Always **temperature-compensated to 25 °C** (≈ 2 %/°C typical).
- ✅ Key drawback: **non-selective** (responds to all ions).

### 🎯 Cross-Cutting Quick Facts
- ✅ **Fastest response:** Spectroscopic (ms–s).
- ✅ **Lowest cost:** Conductivity.
- ✅ **Highest selectivity:** Chromatography.
- ✅ **Highest sensitivity:** Spectroscopy (ppb achievable).
- ✅ **Universal in aqueous streams:** Conductivity.
- ✅ **KTU CO4 mapping:** All three families → analyzing process streams → measuring composition → feedback to control loop.
- ✅ **Sample conditioning** (filtering, pressure reduction, temperature control) is essential **before** all analyzers.

> **END OF NOTES — PECHT414 | Module 4 | Composition Analysis**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Developments of P&I Diagram for Flow Systems, Level, pH Control, Temp Control, Heat Exchangers, Distillation Column, CSTR, Single Effect Evaporator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT414 - PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Developments of P&I Diagram for Flow Systems, Level, pH Control, Temp Control, Heat Exchangers, Distillation Column, CSTR, Single Effect Evaporator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of P&I Diagrams

## 1.1 What is a P&I Diagram?

A **Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&I Diagram / P&ID)** is a detailed graphical schematic that documents the functional relationships between piping, process equipment, instrumentation, and control systems in an industrial process plant. It is the **single most important engineering document** used for design, construction, operation, maintenance, and regulatory compliance (OSHA, ISA, ISO 14617).

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PECHT414)**, students are expected to *develop* (not just read) P&I diagrams for common unit operations, applying the **ISA-5.1 Instrumentation Symbols and Identification** standard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4)**: Students must be able to **develop** complete P&I diagrams from a process description for: Flow, Level, pH, Temperature, Heat Exchanger, Distillation Column, CSTR, and Single Effect Evaporator. Just memorising symbols is **not sufficient** — you must demonstrate loop continuity and correct tagging.

## 1.2 ISA Tag Identification System (ISA-5.1)

Every instrument in a P&ID is identified by a unique **tag number** consisting of letters and a number.

**Format:** `XYY-NNN`

| Position | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **1st letter (X)** | Measured Variable (e.g., T = Temperature, F = Flow, L = Level, P = Pressure, A = Analysis) | **T**IC-101 |
| **2nd/3rd letters (YY)** | Modifier / Function (I = Indicate, C = Control, R = Record, T = Transmit) | **IC** in TIC |
| **Number (NNN)** | Loop number (3 digits) | **101** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Rule**: A tag like `FIC-101` is read as **"Flow Indicating Controller of Loop 101"**. The first letter after the loop number is **always** the *measured variable* — this is a common KTU exam trap.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Orchestra" View

Imagine an industrial plant is an **orchestra**, and the P&ID is the **musical score**:

- **Pipes** = The instrument strings (carrying the "music" = process fluid).
- **Transmitters (TT, FT, LT, AT)** = Microphones placed on each section.
- **Controllers (TIC, FIC, LIC)** = The conductors that listen and adjust.
- **Control Valves (TV, FV, LV)** = The musicians playing louder or softer.
- **Signal lines (dashed)** = The wire from microphone to conductor to musician.

> A **P&ID** shows how the microphone (transmitter) hears the process, sends a signal to the conductor (controller), who then tells the musician (valve) to play. The student must be able to **draw this chain** for any unit operation.

## 1.4 ISA Symbology — Quick Reference

| Symbol | Meaning | KTU Exam Cue |
|---|---|---|
| Circle (no line through) | Field-mounted instrument (e.g., local gauge) | `PT` on the pipe |
| Circle with **horizontal line through top half** | Shared display, shared control | Common in DCS |
| Circle inside a **square** | PLC / DCS mounted | `TIC` on HMI |
| **Solid line** | Process pipe / mechanical link | Carries fluid |
| **Dashed line** with arrowhead | Pneumatic / electric / signal | "4-20 mA signal" |
| **Dashed line with crossed slashes** | Capillary (filled system) | For RTD bulbs |
| **Crossed diagonals (bowtie)** | Discrete signal / binary | ON/OFF |
| **Bubbled tag (e.g., (TIC))** | Computer / DCS function | Software block |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISA-5.1 Standard Line Conventions** (commonly tested as 3-mark questions):
> - **Single horizontal line through a circle** = Instrument is on the **primary process variable**.
> - **Square around a circle** = Function is performed by a **computer / DCS**.
> - **No line through circle, located on the pipe** = **Locally mounted** indicator (no signal shared).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Standard Control Loop

Every P&ID control loop (regardless of variable) contains **four essential elements**, connected in a *series signal chain*:

1. **Sensor / Primary Element** — senses the process (e.g., orifice plate, RTD, DP cell, pH probe).
2. **Transmitter (T)** — converts sensor output to a standard signal (**4-20 mA** or **3-15 psig** pneumatic).
3. **Controller (IC / I/P)** — compares measured value (PV) with setpoint (SP), outputs corrective signal.
4. **Final Control Element (Valve)** — adjusts the manipulated variable (e.g., steam flow, reagent flow).

> The signal chain on a P&ID is drawn as a **dashed line** running from sensor → transmitter → controller → valve. This is the "loop" you must trace in every question.

## 2.2 Standard Signal Conventions

| Signal Type | P&ID Line Style | Range | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pneumatic | Dashed line, small circle at end | **3-15 psig** (20-100 kPa) | Legacy plants, intrinsic safety |
| Electronic / Current | Dashed line, solid arrowhead | **4-20 mA DC** | Modern standard, noise-immune |
| Digital / Fieldbus | Dashed line, **double arrowhead** | H1 / FOUNDATION Fieldbus | Multi-drop, two-wire |
| Capillary | Dashed line with **slashed cross** | Hydraulic | RTD / filled-bulb thermometers |
| Hydraulic | Dashed line, **T-junction at end** | 0-100 psi | High-force actuators |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield**: When a P&ID shows a "**I/P**" converter, the loop is **electronic-in / pneumatic-out**. The P&ID will show two signal segments: dashed line (electronic) → I/P block → dashed-with-circles (pneumatic) → valve.

## 2.3 Valve Action Symbols (Critical for Loop Interpretation)

On a P&ID, the **action of a control valve** is shown as a subscript on the tag:

- **TV-101-A** = Air-to-Close (fail-open). On loss of signal, valve **opens** (safe for heating/cooling).
- **TV-101-B** = Air-to-Open (fail-closed). On loss of signal, valve **closes** (safe for blocked-in flow).

> **Selection rule**: Choose the **fail position** that places the process in a *safe state* on instrument air failure. This is a frequent 7-mark sub-question.

## 2.4 P&ID Development Logic — Step-by-Step

The KTU 2024 scheme expects you to develop a P&ID by following this **5-step thought process**:

1. **Identify the control objective** (e.g., "Maintain reactor temperature at 80 °C ± 1 °C").
2. **Select the measured variable and sensor** (e.g., Temperature → RTD in thermowell).
3. **Select the manipulated variable and final element** (e.g., Cooling water flow → globe valve on jacket inlet).
4. **Choose the controller action** (Direct / Reverse) based on process gain sign.
5. **Decide signal type, location (field/DCS), and interlocks** (e.g., high-temperature trip → shutdown steam).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Loop Performance Equations

These equations govern the P&ID loop behaviour, often tested in derivation (7-mark) questions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e(t) &= SP - PV(t) \\
u(t) &= K_c \, e(t) + \frac{K_c}{\tau_I}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_c \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

where $K_c$ is the controller gain, $\tau_I$ is the integral time, and $\tau_D$ is the derivative time.

**Controller Action Selection Formula:**

$$
\text{Loop Gain} = K_p \cdot K_v \cdot K_c
$$

> For **negative feedback** (stable loop): $K_p \cdot K_v \cdot K_c < 0$, hence controller action must be **opposite** to the process-valve gain product.

| Gain Product $K_p \cdot K_v$ | Required Controller Action | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Positive** (PV rises → MV must rise) | **Direct** (Increase SP → Increase Output) | Level control on outlet |
| **Negative** (PV rises → MV must fall) | **Reverse** (Increase SP → Decrease Output) | Temperature control via cooling water |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

P&IDs developed in Module 4 are the **operational backbone** of:
- **Plant commissioning** (loop checks, SAT/FAT).
- **HAZOP studies** (deviations in flow, level, temp are examined node by node).
- **SIS / Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** assessment — every transmitter feeding a safety function must be traceable on the P&ID.
- **DCS / PLC configuration** — control strategy is built *directly* from the P&ID.
- **Regulatory audits** under OSHA PSM, IEC 61511, and IS 14617.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Sign of Controller Action from Process Gain

**Given**: A heat exchanger outlet temperature $T$ is controlled by manipulating steam flow $F_s$ via control valve $V$. Process gain $K_p = \partial T / \partial F_s > 0$ (more steam → higher $T$). Valve gain $K_v > 0$ (more signal → more steam).

**Step 1**: For the loop to be **negatively fed back**, the controller must *decrease* its output when the PV *increases* above SP.

$$
\Delta u = K_c \cdot (SP - PV) = -K_c \cdot \Delta PV
$$

**Step 2**: For PV to *decrease*, the manipulated variable must *decrease*:

$$
\Delta F_s = K_v \cdot \Delta u < 0
$$

**Step 3**: For PV to *decrease* when $F_s$ decreases, the process gain must be **positive**:

$$
\Delta T = K_p \cdot \Delta F_s < 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad K_p > 0
$$

**Step 4**: Combining:

$$
K_p \cdot K_v \cdot K_c < 0
$$

With $K_p > 0$ and $K_v > 0$, we require $K_c < 0$ → **Reverse Action**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Result**: For a heating loop (steam control), the temperature controller must be set to **REVERSE** action. This derivation is a **guaranteed 7-mark question** in KTU 2024.

## 3.2 Derivation: P&ID Signal Tracing for Cascade Control

A **cascade control loop** (frequently used in distillation and heat exchangers) has two controllers in series: the **master (primary)** controls the main variable, and its output becomes the **setpoint** of the **slave (secondary)** controller.

**Step 1 — Define variables**:
- $y_1$ = Primary (master) variable (e.g., tray temperature)
- $y_2$ = Secondary (slave) variable (e.g., steam flow)
- $r$ = Primary setpoint
- $d$ = Disturbance (e.g., feed composition)

**Step 2 — Master controller output**:

$$
u_1(t) = K_{c1}\!\left[\,r - y_1(t)\,\right] + \frac{K_{c1}}{\tau_{I1}}\!\int_0^t\!\bigl[r - y_1(\tau)\bigr]\,d\tau
$$

**Step 3 — Setpoint of slave**:

$$
SP_2(t) = u_1(t)
$$

**Step 4 — Slave controller output** (drives the valve):

$$
u_2(t) = K_{c2}\!\left[\,SP_2(t) - y_2(t)\,\right] + \frac{K_{c2}}{\tau_{I2}}\!\int_0^t\!\bigl[SP_2(\tau) - y_2(\tau)\bigr]\,d\tau
$$

**Step 5 — Valve position**:

$$
m(t) = f\!\bigl(u_2(t)\bigr), \quad f = \text{installed characteristic curve}
$$

> The P&ID for a cascade loop shows **two control blocks**, **two PV signals**, and a **dashed line from primary controller output → secondary controller setpoint input** (this inter-controller line is the key feature examiners look for).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: P&ID Loop Development for All Module-4 Unit Operations

| # | Unit Operation | Measured Variable (PV) | Sensor / Primary Element | Transmitter Tag | Manipulated Variable | Final Element | Controller Action | KTU 2024 Typical Configuration |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Flow Control (Pipe)** | Volumetric / mass flow | Orifice plate, magnetic, Coriolis, vortex | `FT` | Line flow | Globe / butterfly valve | **Reverse** (more flow → close valve) | Single loop; often cascaded to DP |
| 2 | **Level Control (Tank)** | Liquid level | DP cell, displacer, ultrasonic, capacitance, guided wave radar | `LT` | Outlet flow (most common) | Valve on drain | **Direct** (level up → open drain) | Single loop; may use split-range |
| 3 | **pH Control (Reactor / Neutralizer)** | pH (0-14) | Glass electrode / combination probe | `AT` (Analysis Transmitter) | Reagent flow (acid/base) | On-off or linear valve | **Reverse** for acid addition; **Direct** for base | **Highly nonlinear**; often uses gain-scheduled or feedback + feedforward |
| 4 | **Temperature Control (Jacketed Vessel)** | Process temperature | RTD (Pt100) or Type-J thermocouple in thermowell | `TT` | Steam / cooling water flow | Globe valve | **Reverse** (T up → close steam) | Often **cascade** to jacket temp |
| 5 | **Heat Exchanger (Shell & Tube)** | Process outlet temperature | RTD in thermowell | `TT` | Steam / hot utility flow | Control valve on steam | **Reverse** | **Cascade**: master on process outlet T, slave on steam flow |
| 6 | **Distillation Column** | Tray temperature (sensitive tray) | RTD at tray 5-8 (or near product draw) | `TT` | Reflux flow OR reboiler steam | Reflux valve (R) or steam valve (S) | **Reverse** (T up → less steam) | **Dual composition control**: T tray controls reflux, T bottom controls reboiler steam |
| 7 | **CSTR (Exothermic)** | Reactor temperature | RTD in thermowell | `TT` | Coolant flow in jacket | Valve on coolant | **Reverse** | **Cascade** to jacket temperature; emergency **open** cooling water on high-high T |
| 8 | **Single Effect Evaporator** | Vapour space pressure (or liquor level) | Pressure transmitter `PT` / level transmitter `LT` | `PT` / `LT` | Steam flow (vapour line) / feed flow | Steam valve, condensate valve | **Reverse** for pressure control | Often combines **pressure, level, and feed flow** in split-range |

## 3.4 Worked Example: Heat Exchanger P&ID Derivation (Full 14-Mark Format)

**Problem Statement**: A shell-and-tube heat exchanger cools a process stream from **90 °C to 50 °C** using cooling water on the shell side. The cooling water supply pressure is **4 bar** and the flow is currently set manually. Develop a P&ID with **cascade temperature control** so that the outlet process temperature is held at **50 °C ± 1 °C** despite ±10 % disturbances in inlet temperature.

**Step 1 — Identify control objective**:
$$
T_{out,process} = 50\,^\circ\text{C} \pm 1\,^\circ\text{C}
$$

**Step 2 — Select sensors**:
- Primary sensor: **Pt100 RTD** in thermowell on process outlet.
- Secondary sensor: **Magnetic flow meter** on cooling water return line (slave loop).

**Step 3 — Select final element**: Air-to-**open** globe valve on cooling water inlet (fail-closed, safe — no cooling on instrument air loss would overheat downstream, but interlocks trip the feed).

> For an *exothermic* downstream reactor, the valve would be **fail-open** (cooling water continues on air loss).

**Step 4 — Determine controller action**:
- Process gain: $\partial T_{out}/\partial F_{cw} < 0$ (more cooling → lower $T_{out}$).
- Valve gain: $K_v < 0$ (more signal → close A/O valve) — or $K_v > 0$ (A/C valve).
- For negative feedback: $K_c \cdot K_p \cdot K_v < 0$.

**Step 5 — Cascade configuration**:
- **Master**: TIC-101 (PT100 input, output drives setpoint of FIC-102).
- **Slave**: FIC-102 (magnetic flow input, output drives valve).

> This same logic applies to **distillation column** (master on tray T → slave on reflux flow or steam flow) and **CSTR** (master on reactor T → slave on coolant flow).

## 3.5 P&ID Safety & Interlock Conventions (Module 4 Specific)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mandatory Additions to Every P&ID**:
> 1. **High-High (HH) interlock** on reactor/evaporator/column temperature — must trip feed and add emergency cooling.
> 2. **Low-Low (LL) interlock** on reboiler level — must close steam to prevent dry firing.
> 3. **Bypass valve around the control valve** (with double block) for maintenance isolation.
> 4. **Manual loading station** on every controller (shown as a circle with a hand symbol).
> 5. **Hand-valve** (ball valve) on every instrument tap for isolation.
> 6. **Root valve** symbol on every transmitter connection to process.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a P&ID? List the four essential elements of any feedback control loop shown on it.**

**Model Answer**:
A Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID) is a detailed engineering schematic showing the interconnection of process equipment, piping, control valves, sensors, transmitters, and controllers in a plant, drawn using ISA-5.1 symbology.

The four essential elements of every feedback loop are:
1. **Primary element (sensor)** — senses the process variable (e.g., RTD, orifice plate).
2. **Transmitter (T)** — converts sensor signal to a standard 4-20 mA or 3-15 psig.
3. **Controller (IC)** — compares PV with SP and outputs a corrective signal.
4. **Final control element (control valve)** — adjusts the manipulated variable.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between the ISA symbols for a field-mounted instrument and a DCS-mounted instrument. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer**:
- A **field-mounted instrument** is drawn as a **plain circle** placed on or near the process pipe. Example: `PT-101` shown on the pipe.
- A **DCS / computer-mounted instrument** is drawn as a **circle inside a square** (square = computer function). Example: `TIC-201` shown inside the DCS panel boundary.

Additionally, a **horizontally shared display** (one circle, two horizontal lines) is used when two PVs are shown on a single HMI faceplate.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Develop a complete P&ID with proper ISA symbology and tag numbering for a shell-and-tube heat exchanger that cools a process fluid from 90 °C to 50 °C using cooling water on the shell side. Use cascade control with the master on process outlet temperature and the slave on cooling water flow. Specify all sensors, transmitters, controllers, control valves, and at least two safety interlocks.**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1 — Objective & Variable Identification**:
- **Controlled (PV)**: Process outlet temperature, SP = 50 °C.
- **Manipulated (MV)**: Cooling water flow.
- **Disturbance**: ±10 % inlet process temperature variation.

**Step 2 — Sensor Selection**:
- **Primary sensor**: Pt100 RTD in thermowell on process outlet → Tag `TE-401`.
- **Secondary sensor**: Magnetic flow meter on cooling water return line → Tag `FE-402`.

**Step 3 — Transmitters**:
- `TT-401` — temperature transmitter (4-20 mA) on `TE-401`.
- `FT-402` — flow transmitter (4-20 mA) on `FE-402`.

**Step 4 — Controllers**:
- `TIC-401` (master, DCS-mounted) — receives PV from `TT-401`, SP = 50 °C.
- `FIC-402` (slave, DCS-mounted) — receives PV from `FT-402`, SP = output of `TIC-401` (remote SP link).

**Step 5 — Final Element**:
- `TV-401` — Air-to-Close globe valve on cooling water inlet (fail-closed → no cooling on air loss will be detected by high-temperature interlock).

**Step 6 — Safety Interlocks**:
- `THH-401` — High-High temperature trip on process outlet → trips feed and opens emergency cooling water solenoid valve.
- `FT-403` — Low cooling water flow interlock → trips upstream process feed pump.

**Step 7 — Manual / Maintenance Provisions**:
- Hand-valves (ball valves) on each instrument tap.
- Bypass loop around `TV-401` with double-block valves.
- Manual loading station on `TIC-401` and `FIC-402`.

**Step 8 — Signal Flow on P&ID** (dashed lines):
`TE-401 → TT-401 → TIC-401 → (remote SP) → FIC-402 → I/P → TV-401 → Cooling water → HX`

**Final Tag List**:

| Tag | Function | Location |
|---|---|---|
| `TE-401` | RTD sensing element | Field |
| `TT-401` | Temperature transmitter | Field |
| `TIC-401` | Master controller | DCS |
| `FE-402` | Flow element (magnetic) | Field |
| `FT-402` | Flow transmitter | Field |
| `FIC-402` | Slave controller | DCS |
| `TV-401` | Cooling water valve | Field |
| `THH-401` | High-High interlock | DCS |
| `FT-403` | Low-flow interlock | DCS |

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**For the cascade loop above, determine the controller actions (direct/reverse) of TIC-401 and FIC-402. Justify with the sign of process gain and valve characteristic.**

**Model Solution**:

**TIC-401 (Master, on outlet temperature)**:
- Process gain $K_p = \partial T_{out}/\partial F_{cw} < 0$ (more cooling water → lower $T_{out}$).
- Slave loop (FIC-402) is tuned to keep $F_{cw}$ at the master SP — for the master, the effective inner-loop gain is positive ($K_{inner} > 0$).
- Combined master loop gain: $K_c \cdot K_{inner} \cdot K_p < 0$ for stability.
- Since $K_{inner} > 0$ and $K_p < 0$, we need $K_c > 0$ → **REVERSE action**.

> Reverse action definition: Increase in PV above SP → Decrease in controller output → Master reduces SP to slave → Slave reduces flow → PV drops back. Stable.

**FIC-402 (Slave, on cooling water flow)**:
- Process gain: $\partial F_{cw}/\partial u_{valve} > 0$ (more signal → more flow).
- Valve gain: A/C valve, $K_v < 0$ (more signal → closer valve → less flow).
- For stability: $K_c \cdot K_p \cdot K_v < 0$.
- With $K_p > 0$ and $K_v < 0$, we need $K_c > 0$ → **REVERSE action**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Where students lose 3-5 marks)**:
> 1. **Confusing "direct" with "fail-open"** — they are unrelated. Direct/Reverse refers to controller sign; fail-open/fail-closed refers to valve air action.
> 2. **Forgetting to define $K_p$, $K_v$ explicitly** before deriving $K_c$ sign — examiners award 2 marks for this alone.
> 3. **Assuming the slave loop must be reverse** — for a heating loop, the slave is *direct*. Always derive, never memorise.
> 4. **Not mentioning instrument air failure direction** (fail-safe position) — worth 1 mark on the valve sub-question.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Develop a P&ID for the temperature control of an exothermic CSTR using a cascade control strategy. The reactor is to operate at 80 °C with cooling water in the jacket. The reaction is highly exothermic and runaway is a serious safety concern. Specify all instruments, valves, and an emergency interlock strategy.**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1 — Control Objective**: Maintain reactor contents at 80 °C ± 0.5 °C. Avoid runaway.

**Step 2 — Sensors**:
- **Primary**: `TE-601` — Pt100 RTD in thermowell inside reactor.
- **Secondary**: `TE-602` — Pt100 RTD in jacket outlet.
- **Emergency**: `TE-603` — Independent RTD for high-high interlock (separate thermowell, separate transmitter, separate cable — SIL 1 minimum).

**Step 3 — Transmitters**:
- `TT-601` — Master transmitter (4-20 mA, redundant).
- `TT-602` — Slave transmitter (jacket T).
- `TT-603` — Interlock transmitter (independent).

**Step 4 — Controllers**:
- `TIC-601` — Master controller (reactor T) on DCS, reverse action.
- `TIC-602` — Slave controller (jacket T) on DCS, remote SP from `TIC-601`.

**Step 5 — Valves**:
- `TV-601` — Air-to-Open cooling water valve (fail-open → on instrument air failure, **maximum cooling** is delivered — this is the correct fail-safe direction for an exothermic reaction).
- `XV-601` — Emergency solenoid (3-way), normally open, energize-to-close.

**Step 6 — Interlocks**:
- `THH-601` — High-High (85 °C) on reactor T → Trip reactor feed (`XV-602`), close steam to jacket, open emergency chilled water (`XV-603`).
- `LSL-601` — Low-low level interlock → close `TV-601` to prevent dry jacket.
- `TT-603` (independent) → wired directly to ESD (Emergency Shutdown) relay — bypasses DCS.

**Step 7 — Additional Safety**:
- Burst disc `PSV-601` on reactor (mechanical, last line of defence).
- `FT-604` on cooling water return → low-flow alarm.

**Step 8 — P&ID Signal Flow**:
```
TE-601 → TT-601 → TIC-601 → (remote SP) → TIC-602 → I/P → TV-601 → CW → Jacket → Reactor
TE-602 → TT-602 → TIC-602
TE-603 → TT-603 → ESD relay → XV-602 (close feed), XV-603 (open chilled water)
```

**Tag List**:

| Tag | Function | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `TE/TT-601` | Master reactor T | DCS, reverse action |
| `TE/TT-602` | Slave jacket T | DCS, reverse action |
| `TE/TT-603` | Independent Hi-Hi T | ESD, SIL 1 |
| `TIC-601` | Master controller | Reverse |
| `TIC-602` | Slave controller | Reverse |
| `TV-601` | CW valve (A/O) | Fail-open |
| `XV-602` | Feed trip valve | De-energize to trip |
| `XV-603` | Emergency chilled water | Energize to open |
| `THH-601` | High-high alarm | DCS + ESD |
| `LSL-601` | Low-low level switch | ESD |

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**A distillation column is to be controlled using dual temperature cascade — one temperature controller on a tray near the top (controls reflux flow) and one on the reboiler (controls steam flow). Develop the P&ID, and explain the role of feedforward compensation if the feed composition changes rapidly.**

**Model Solution**:

**Step 1 — P&ID Development**:
- **Top loop (Reflux Control)**:
  - `TE-501` RTD on tray 5 (sensitive tray near top).
  - `TT-501` transmitter → `TIC-501` master controller (reverse action).
  - `TIC-501` output → remote SP of `FIC-501` (slave, on reflux flow).
  - `FIC-501` output → `FV-501` (reflux valve) on reflux line from condenser reflux drum.
- **Bottom loop (Reboiler Steam Control)**:
  - `TE-502` RTD in reboiler (or bottom tray).
  - `TT-502` → `TIC-502` master (reverse action).
  - `TIC-502` output → `FIC-502` slave on steam flow.
  - `FIC-502` → `FV-502` steam valve.
  - **Override**: `LIC-503` (reboiler level) — if level drops, reduces steam setpoint to prevent dry firing.

**Step 2 — Feedforward Compensation**:
A change in **feed composition $z_F$** is detected by an **online composition analyser (`AT-501`)** or inferred from a feed **temperature transmitter** and **flow transmitter (`FT-501`)**.

The feedforward signal is computed as:
$$
F_{R,FF} = K_{FF} \cdot F_{feed} \cdot (z_{F,ref} - z_F)
$$
where $K_{FF}$ is the steady-state feedforward gain, $z_{F,ref}$ is the reference feed composition, and $F_{R,FF}$ is the feedforward adjustment to the reflux setpoint.

On the P&ID, the feedforward is shown as a **dashed signal line from `AT-501` and `FT-501` directly into the summing junction before `TIC-501`** (or as a bias added to its output).

**Step 3 — Why Feedforward?**
Tray temperature is a *delayed* indicator of composition change. A feedflow + composition change occurring upstream would take 5-15 minutes to propagate to tray 5. Feedforward acts **immediately** on detection, while the temperature cascade corrects for any steady-state error.

**Step 4 — Controller Actions (Summary)**:
- `TIC-501`, `TIC-502`: **Reverse** (tray T up → less reflux / less steam).
- `FIC-501`, `FIC-502`: **Reverse** (signal up → close valve).
- `LIC-503` (reboiler level): **Direct** (level up → close steam).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall on Distillation P&IDs (Commonly loses 4 marks)**:
> 1. **Forgetting the reboiler level override** — examiners deduct 2 marks if `LIC-503` is missing, as dry-firing is a real industrial hazard.
> 2. **Drawing the reflux line as the feed to the column** — reflux should originate from the **reflux drum** outlet, not from the condenser outlet.
> 3. **Failing to identify the sensitive tray** — students often mark a tray near the feed (incorrect) instead of tray 5-8 near the product draw where $\partial T/\partial z$ is steepest.
> 4. **Showing only ONE temperature controller** — dual temperature control is mandatory for the 14-mark KTU question.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 P&ID Development**

- [ ] **ISA-5.1** is the mandatory symbol standard (circle = instrument, square = DCS, line through = primary variable).
- [ ] **First letter of tag = measured variable**; subsequent letters = function (I=Indicate, C=Control, R=Record, T=Transmit).
- [ ] **Signal types**: 4-20 mA (electronic), 3-15 psig (pneumatic), Fieldbus (digital), Capillary (thermal), Hydraulic (mechanical).
- [ ] **Standard signal chain**: Sensor (E) → Transmitter (T) → Controller (IC) → I/P (if pneumatic valve) → Valve → Process.
- [ ] **Cascade control** uses **two controllers in series**; output of master = setpoint of slave; always draw the inter-controller dashed line.
- [ ] **Controller action sign** is determined from the inequality $K_c \cdot K_p \cdot K_v < 0$ for negative feedback stability.
- [ ] **Fail-safe direction**: Heating loops → A/O (fail-open, keep heating); Exothermic reactors → A/C (fail-closed, stop heating) OR A/O cooling valve (keep cooling on air loss).
- [ ] **Mandatory safety add-ons** to every P&ID: HH/LL interlocks, root valves, hand-valves, bypass, manual loading station.
- [ ] **pH control** is highly nonlinear — use gain-scheduling or feedforward from reagent flow.
- [ ] **Distillation columns** require **dual temperature control** (reflux + reboiler steam) plus a level override on the reboiler.
- [ ] **CSTR control** must use a **cascade** (reactor T → jacket T) with an **independent** high-high interlock on a separate transmitter (SIL requirement).
- [ ] **Heat exchangers** use **cascade** (outlet T → utility flow) with the slave fast and the master slow.
- [ ] **Single effect evaporator** uses **pressure control** (vapour space) as primary, with level and feed flow as secondary loops, plus a dry-fire trip on level.
- [ ] **P&ID is a complete document** — partial loops with missing sensors or valves are awarded **zero** in KTU valuation.
- [ ] **Drawing conventions**: Solid line = pipe; Dashed line = signal; Dashed with circles = pneumatic; Dashed with slashes = capillary; Bowtie = discrete/binary.

> **Final KTU 2024 Tip**: For a 14-mark "develop the P&ID" question, allocate marks as: **Objective (1) + Sensors (1) + Transmitters (1) + Controllers (1) + Valve selection (1) + Action derivation (2) + Interlocks (2) + Tag list (1) + Sketch quality (2) + Loop continuity proof (2)** = 14 marks.
